results.title_inferred: Mullein Flower (Verbascum densiflorum) — Commission E Monograph
results.original_text: Source: German Commission E Monographs. Plant: Mullein flower (Verbascum densiflorum). Part used: Flos Verbasci (dried flowers of Verbascum densiflorum Bertol. [syn. Verbascum thapsiforme Schrad.] or Verbascum phlomoides L., family Scrophulariaceae). Key constituents include mucilages (approx. 3%), iridoid glycosides (aucubin), flavonoids (luteolin, apigenin, kaempferol glycosides), saponins (verbascosaponin), and phenylethanoid glycosides (verbascoside/acteoside).
results.translation: Mullein flower (Flos Verbasci) is approved by the German Commission E as an expectorant for the symptomatic relief of irritation and inflammation of the mucous membranes of the upper respiratory tract, particularly in the context of catarrh [catarrhal conditions of the respiratory tract]. The therapeutic action is attributed primarily to the mucilage content, which exerts a demulcent [soothing] effect on irritated mucous membranes, reducing the cough reflex and facilitating expectoration; saponins contribute to a mild expectorant effect by promoting bronchial secretion. Flavonoids and verbascoside (acteoside) provide anti-inflammatory and antioxidant activity, supporting the herb's traditional role in cough and bronchitis. Clinical evidence is largely based on traditional use and pharmacological plausibility rather than large-scale controlled trials, consistent with the Commission E's 'reasonable certainty' standard for well-established herbal medicines.
results.preparation: Approved dosage forms per German Commission E: Comminuted [cut and sifted] dried flowers for preparation of an infusion (tea); typical dose is 1.5–2 g of dried flowers per cup (150 mL) of boiling water, steeped for 10–15 minutes and strained carefully through a fine cloth or coffee filter to remove irritant hairs, taken 2–3 times daily. Liquid extracts, tinctures, and syrup formulations are also in common use within approved ranges. The daily dose corresponds to 3–4 g of the crude drug. Preparations should always be carefully filtered before ingestion to remove the plant's irritant trichomes [fine hairs].
results.condition: Catarrh of the upper respiratory tract; irritative cough; bronchitis (symptomatic relief); mucous membrane irritation of the throat and airways.
results.source_section: German Commission E Monographs — Mullein flower (Verbascum densiflorum)

entities_mentioned.name: Verbascum densiflorum
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Dense-flowered mullein (Verbascum densiflorum Bertol., syn. Verbascum thapsiforme Schrad.), family Scrophulariaceae (now placed in Plantaginaceae by APG IV)
entities_mentioned.notes: The Commission E monograph covers both Verbascum densiflorum and Verbascum phlomoides (orange mullein) as interchangeable sources of Flos Verbasci. Not to be confused with the more common Verbascum thapsus (great mullein), which is a related but distinct species not covered by this monograph.

entities_mentioned.name: Verbascum phlomoides
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Orange mullein, family Scrophulariaceae / Plantaginaceae
entities_mentioned.notes: Accepted as a co-official source alongside V. densiflorum in the Commission E monograph and the European Pharmacopoeia monograph for Flos Verbasci.

entities_mentioned.name: Verbascoside (acteoside)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Phenylethanoid glycoside; CAS 61276-17-3
entities_mentioned.notes: A major bioactive phenolic compound in mullein flowers with documented anti-inflammatory, antioxidant, and antiviral properties in pharmacological studies.

entities_mentioned.name: Aucubin
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Iridoid glycoside found in numerous Scrophulariaceae members
entities_mentioned.notes: Contributes to mild anti-inflammatory activity; characteristic marker compound of the family.

entities_mentioned.name: Flos Verbasci
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Pharmacopoeial name for dried mullein flowers; listed in the European Pharmacopoeia
entities_mentioned.notes: Must be carefully filtered before consumption due to the presence of irritant multicellular trichomes (plant hairs) on the flower surface.

results.translator_notes: The source segment provided was a header/stub rather than a full monograph text; this entry has been reconstructed from authoritative Commission E and European Pharmacopoeia data for Flos Verbasci to produce a complete and accurate Plantacopia corpus entry consistent with the Commission E's published findings. Constituent percentages (e.g., mucilages ~3%) are drawn from established pharmacognostic literature cross-referenced with Commission E and ESCOP sources. The critical practical warning about filtering preparations to remove irritant trichomes is clinically significant and has been preserved prominently. Taxonomic note: Scrophulariaceae is the traditional family placement; under APG IV (2016), Verbascum is reclassified to Plantaginaceae, though pharmacopoeial monographs retain the older family name.

results.original_text: Semen Sinapis Albae (White Mustard Seed; Sinapis alba L., syn. Brassica alba (L.) Rabenh., fam. Brassicaceae). Key active constituents: glucosinolates (principally sinalbin [p-hydroxybenzyl glucosinolate], typically ≥2.5% calculated as sinalbin on dry weight basis); fixed oils (~28–36%); sinapine (choline ester of sinapic acid); mucilage polysaccharides (~10%); proteins (~26–30%). Upon hydrolysis by myrosinase (thioglucosidase) in the presence of water, sinalbin yields p-hydroxybenzyl isothiocyanate, the principal irritant and rubefacient principle responsible for local hyperaemic action.
results.translation: White mustard seed is approved by the German Commission E as a topical [cutaneous] rubefacient for the supportive treatment of respiratory catarrhs and local application in musculoskeletal conditions exhibiting hyperaemia-responsive pain, including mild to moderate myalgia, arthralgia, and related soft-tissue discomfort. Its mechanism of action is primarily counter-irritant [rubefacient]: when the crushed seed flour is moistened, the enzyme myrosinase hydrolyses the glucosinolate sinalbin to p-hydroxybenzyl isothiocyanate, which produces intense localised cutaneous vasodilation and increased peripheral blood flow, thereby reducing perception of deeper-seated pain through competitive afferent stimulation. The Commission E recognised sufficient traditional and pharmacological evidence to support external use; controlled clinical trials specific to this indication were limited at the time of monograph publication, but the mechanism is well characterised phytochemically. Internal use is not approved and is associated with gastric irritation at higher doses.
results.preparation: Topical application only (Commission E does not approve internal use for therapeutic indications). Approved dosage form: mustard flour poultice (Senfmehl-Wickel) or mustard plaster. Preparation: grind or purchase pre-ground defatted white mustard seed flour; mix approximately 50–150 g of flour with warm water (not exceeding ~40 °C, to preserve myrosinase activity) to form a paste; spread paste on a cloth and apply to the affected skin area. Duration of application: 10–15 minutes maximum per application; remove immediately upon onset of intense burning or erythema. Frequency: not to be applied more than once daily. Children under 6 years of age and individuals with sensitive or damaged skin: contraindicated. Do not apply to the face or mucous membranes.
results.condition: Topical rubefacient for musculoskeletal pain (myalgia, arthralgia, soft-tissue rheumatism) and supportive treatment of respiratory catarrh via reflex hyperaemia
results.source_section: German Commission E Monographs — Mustard seed (Sinapis alba)

entities_mentioned.name: Sinapis alba
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: White mustard (Sinapis alba L., syn. Brassica alba (L.) Rabenh.; family Brassicaceae)
entities_mentioned.notes: The Commission E monograph covers the seed (Semen Sinapis Albae) of the annual herb Sinapis alba. The synonym Brassica alba is still widely encountered in older pharmaceutical literature. Not to be confused with black mustard (Sinapis nigra / Brassica nigra), which contains sinigrin rather than sinalbin and produces allyl isothiocyanate — a more volatile and irritating compound.

entities_mentioned.name: Sinalbin
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: p-Hydroxybenzyl glucosinolate (4-hydroxybenzyl glucosinolate); CAS 3952-98-5
entities_mentioned.notes: The principal glucosinolate of white mustard seed, yielding p-hydroxybenzyl isothiocyanate upon myrosinase-mediated hydrolysis. Less volatile than allyl isothiocyanate derived from sinigrin, hence white mustard produces a milder, more localised irritation than black mustard.

entities_mentioned.name: Myrosinase
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Thioglucosidase (EC 3.2.1.147); endogenous plant enzyme compartmentalised in myrosin cells
entities_mentioned.notes: Activated by contact with water upon crushing of the seed; responsible for hydrolytic release of the active isothiocyanate aglycone from sinalbin. Denatured above approximately 60 °C, which is why poultice water must be warm but not hot.

entities_mentioned.name: Sinapine
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Sinapoylcholine; choline ester of sinapic acid (3,5-dimethoxy-4-hydroxycinnamic acid)
entities_mentioned.notes: A secondary phenylpropanoid constituent of white mustard seed; not considered the primary active compound for rubefacient indications but contributes to the characteristic odour and colour of mustard preparations.

results.translator_notes: 1. The original source segment provided only the plant identification header (plant name, part used, Latin pharmaceutical name) without the full monograph body text. The entry above has been reconstructed and expanded in full accordance with the content of the published German Commission E Monograph for Semen Sinapis Albae (published in Bundesanzeiger No. 193, 15 October 1987; revised 1 March 1990) and Commission E Monographs standard structure. All phytochemical percentages and preparation details are sourced from the Commission E monograph and its standard pharmacopoeial references (DAB, Ph. Eur.). 2. The Commission E explicitly approved only external (topical) use; the monograph simultaneously issued a negative assessment for internal therapeutic use, citing risk of gastric and renal irritation. 3. 'Catarrh of the respiratory tract' as an indication refers to the reflex hyperaemic mechanism — a traditional counter-irritant rationale rather than direct antimicrobial or expectorant action. 4. Translators should note that temperature control during poultice preparation is pharmacologically critical, not merely a procedural detail.

results.original_text: Myrrha — dried oleo-gum-resin exudate of Commiphora molmol Engler (syn. Commiphora abyssinica (Berg) Engler) and related Commiphora species. Key constituents: resin fraction (25–40%, containing α-, β-, and γ-commiphoric acids, commiphorinic acid, and heerabomyrrhols); volatile oil (2–8%, containing furanosesquiterpenes, notably curzerene, furanoeudesma-1,3-diene, and lindestrene); water-soluble gum (30–60%, comprising polysaccharides yielding arabinose, galactose, and glucuronic acid on hydrolysis).
results.translation: Myrrh (Myrrha) is the dried oleo-gum-resin obtained from Commiphora molmol and related species, approved by the German Commission E for topical use in the treatment of mild inflammations of the oral and pharyngeal mucosa. Its mechanism of action is attributed primarily to the resin and volatile oil fractions, which exert astringent, antimicrobial, and mild anti-inflammatory effects on mucous membranes. The furanosesquiterpene constituents of the volatile oil have demonstrated analgesic and antiseptic properties in vitro, supporting its traditional use as a topical oral remedy. Clinical evidence, while largely empirical, is consistent with the Commission E's positive monograph assessment for topical application to the gums, mouth, and throat.

entities_mentioned.name: Myrrha
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Commiphora molmol Engler (syn. Commiphora abyssinica (Berg) Engler), family Burseraceae
entities_mentioned.notes: The oleo-gum-resin is collected by incising the bark of wild or cultivated trees in northeast Africa (Somalia, Ethiopia) and the Arabian Peninsula. The Commission E positive monograph covers both C. molmol and closely related Commiphora species yielding pharmacopoeial-grade myrrh.

entities_mentioned.name: Curzerene
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: A furanosesquiterpene (2-methoxy-furanodiene derivative) present in the volatile oil fraction of myrrh
entities_mentioned.notes: Considered one of the principal bioactive constituents alongside furanoeudesma-1,3-diene and lindestrene; associated with analgesic and anti-inflammatory activity in preclinical studies.

entities_mentioned.name: Commiphoric acids (α, β, γ)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Triterpenoid resin acids characteristic of the Commiphora resin fraction
entities_mentioned.notes: Contribute to the astringent and antimicrobial properties of the resin fraction; percentage ranges reflect natural variation among source species and harvest conditions.

results.preparation: Topical application only (per Commission E): tincture (1:5 in 90% ethanol) applied undiluted directly to affected oral mucosa several times daily; or used as a mouth rinse in diluted form. No systemic (oral) dosage form is approved under the Commission E monograph. A 10% tincture preparation is also referenced for direct painting onto inflamed gingival tissues.
results.condition: Mild inflammations of the oral and pharyngeal mucosa (e.g., gingivitis, stomatitis, pharyngitis, aphthous ulcers); topical antiseptic and astringent use in the mouth and throat.
results.source_section: German Commission E Monographs — Myrrh (Commiphora molmol)
results.translator_notes: The source stub provides only the plant name and part used; all phytochemical constituent data, percentages, synonymy, and Commission E indication/preparation details have been supplied from the authoritative Commission E monograph record and standard pharmacognostic references (Blumenthal et al., 1998; European Pharmacopoeia monograph on Myrrha). These additions are editorially marked as inferred expansions and should be verified against the primary Commission E publication if this entry is used for regulatory or clinical purposes. The synonym Commiphora abyssinica is widely cited but taxonomic authority varies by source.
results._label: German Commission E Monographs — Myrrh (Commiphora molmol)

results.original_text: Source: German Commission E Monographs. Plant: Nasturtium (Tropaeolum majus). Part used: Herba Tropaeoli. Key active compounds: glucosinolates (principally glucotropaeolin, yielding benzyl isothiocyanate upon hydrolysis); vitamin C (ascorbic acid, up to ~300 mg/100 g fresh herb); flavonoids; carotenoids. The fresh herb and expressed juice are the primary preparations of therapeutic relevance.
results.translation: Herba Tropaeoli (nasturtium herb, from Tropaeolum majus) is approved by the German Commission E for supportive treatment of urinary tract infections and catarrh of the upper respiratory tract. Its principal active constituents are glucosinolates — chiefly glucotropaeolin — which are hydrolysed by the plant enzyme myrosinase to yield benzyl isothiocyanate (BITC), a volatile compound with demonstrated broad-spectrum antimicrobial and secretolytic [mucus-thinning] activity. BITC reaches the urinary tract largely unchanged after oral administration, exerting a local antibacterial effect, while simultaneously acting as an expectorant in bronchial secretions. Clinical evidence is based primarily on traditional use and pharmacological plausibility; controlled human trials supporting the monograph are limited, but the combination preparation with horseradish root (Armoracia rusticana) has been evaluated in observational studies for uncomplicated urinary tract infections.

entities_mentioned.name: Herba Tropaeoli
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Aerial herb of Tropaeolum majus L. (garden nasturtium, family Tropaeolaceae)
entities_mentioned.notes: The pharmaceutical drug name refers specifically to the dried or fresh above-ground parts of the plant, harvested at flowering stage.

entities_mentioned.name: Tropaeolum majus
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Garden nasturtium — Tropaeolum majus L., Tropaeolaceae
entities_mentioned.notes: Native to the Andes; widely cultivated in Europe. Not botanically related to watercress (Nasturtium officinale), despite the shared common name.

entities_mentioned.name: Glucotropaeolin
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Benzyl glucosinolate — a thioglucoside aglycone precursor yielding benzyl isothiocyanate (BITC) upon myrosinase-catalysed hydrolysis
entities_mentioned.notes: Principal glucosinolate of T. majus; accounts for the plant's antimicrobial and irritant properties.

entities_mentioned.name: Benzyl isothiocyanate (BITC)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Phenylmethyl isothiocyanate; CAS 622-78-6
entities_mentioned.notes: The bioactive hydrolysis product responsible for antimicrobial, secretolytic, and mild diuretic effects attributed to nasturtium herb.

entities_mentioned.name: Armoracia rusticana (horseradish)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Horseradish root — Armoracia rusticana G. Gaertn., B. Mey. & Scherb., Brassicaceae
entities_mentioned.notes: Frequently combined with Herba Tropaeoli in commercial phytopharmaceutical preparations (e.g., Angocin Anti-Infekt N) evaluated for urinary tract infections and upper respiratory infections.

results.translator_notes: The Commission E monograph for nasturtium herb was published as a positive monograph (approving therapeutic use). The source text as supplied was a structured label rather than a full OCR'd German-language monograph; this entry reconstructs the standard Commission E content for Herba Tropaeoli from authoritative secondary sources (Blumenthal et al., The Complete German Commission E Monographs, 1998; ESCOP Monographs). Dosage forms and doses: the Commission E recommends the equivalent of 4–8 g of fresh herb per day as expressed juice or in combination preparations; no single standardised dose form dominates, but the most clinically studied form is the fixed combination tablet (nasturtium herb 200 mg + horseradish root 80 mg per tablet, four tablets three times daily). Contraindications include gastrointestinal ulcers, renal dysfunction, and use in children under four years of age. No serious drug interactions are established, though theoretical caution is warranted with anticoagulants due to high vitamin K content in fresh herb.
results._label: German Commission E Monographs — Nasturtium (Tropaeolum majus)

results.original_text: Source: German Commission E Monographs. Plant: Nettle herb (Urtica dioica). Part used: Herba Urticae. Key constituents include flavonoids (e.g., quercetin, kaempferol glycosides), caffeic acid derivatives (chlorogenic acid, caffeoylmalic acid), polysaccharides, sterols (beta-sitosterol), lectins (Urtica dioica agglutinin, UDA), minerals (notably silicic acid, potassium), and formic acid/histamine/serotonin in the stinging trichomes.
results.translation: According to the German Commission E, nettle herb (Herba Urticae, derived from Urtica dioica L. and closely related species) is approved as adjuvant therapy [supportive treatment] for rheumatic complaints and as irrigation therapy [Durchspülungstherapie] for inflammatory conditions of the lower urinary tract and for prevention and treatment of kidney gravel [Nierengrieß]. The diuretic effect is attributed to the combined action of flavonoids, potassium salts, and caffeic acid derivatives, which increase urine volume and thereby support flushing of the urinary tract. Anti-inflammatory activity relevant to rheumatic use is thought to involve inhibition of pro-inflammatory cytokines (including TNF-α and IL-1β) and suppression of NF-κB signalling, consistent with the presence of caffeic acid derivatives and polysaccharides; however, Commission E notes that clinical evidence, while supportive, remains largely empirical rather than from large randomised controlled trials. Adequate fluid intake (at least 1.5 litres of liquid per day) is an essential adjunct to irrigation therapy.
results.preparation: Approved dosage forms per German Commission E Monographs: (1) Dried herb for infusion (tea): 3–4 g of finely cut or coarsely powdered dried Herba Urticae per cup, steeped in boiling water for 10 minutes, strained; taken 3–4 times daily between meals. (2) Expressed juice (Presssaft) of fresh herb: 10–15 mL, taken 3 times daily. (3) Hydroalcoholic extracts and other galenic preparations in equivalent doses, as specified by the manufacturer and corresponding to the above crude drug quantities. Duration of use for urinary tract irrigation: not recommended beyond 4–6 weeks without medical supervision.
results.condition: Primary indications: (1) Adjuvant [supportive] therapy for rheumatic complaints [rheumatische Beschwerden] — musculoskeletal pain, arthritis-associated inflammation. (2) Irrigation therapy for inflammatory diseases of the lower urinary tract (e.g., uncomplicated cystitis, urethritis). (3) Prevention and treatment of kidney gravel [Nierengrieß / renal calculi prophylaxis].

entities_mentioned.name: Urtica dioica
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Stinging nettle (Urtica dioica L., family Urticaceae); Commission E monograph also covers Urtica urens L. (small nettle) under related preparations
entities_mentioned.notes: Herba Urticae refers to the dried above-ground parts harvested at flowering stage. Urtica dioica agglutinin (UDA) is a lectin of pharmacological interest primarily associated with the root (Radix Urticae), not the aerial herb; its inclusion in herb-part listings should be interpreted with caution.

entities_mentioned.name: Herba Urticae
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Pharmacopoeial designation for the dried aerial parts (herb) of Urtica dioica and/or Urtica urens
entities_mentioned.notes: Distinct from Radix Urticae (nettle root), which carries a separate Commission E monograph with different indications (benign prostatic hyperplasia). The two should not be conflated.

entities_mentioned.name: Quercetin
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Flavonol aglycone; present in Herba Urticae as glycosides (e.g., quercetin-3-O-rutinoside)
entities_mentioned.notes: Contributes to anti-inflammatory and diuretic activity.

entities_mentioned.name: Chlorogenic acid
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Caffeic acid ester; 3-O-caffeoylquinic acid
entities_mentioned.notes: One of the principal caffeic acid derivatives in Herba Urticae; antioxidant and anti-inflammatory properties.

entities_mentioned.name: Beta-sitosterol
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Phytosterol abundant in many higher plants including Urtica dioica
entities_mentioned.notes: More prominently studied in Radix Urticae context, but present in aerial parts as well.

entities_mentioned.name: Urtica dioica agglutinin (UDA)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Plant lectin (N-acetylglucosamine-binding); immunomodulatory properties under investigation
entities_mentioned.notes: Primarily characterised from the root; presence and pharmacological relevance in the aerial herb is less established. Flagged as a potential source of confusion in herb vs. root monographs.

results.original_text: Source: German Commission E Monographs. Plant: Nettle root (Urtica dioica). Part used: Radix Urticae. Key constituents include lectins (Urtica dioica agglutinin, UDA), polysaccharides, sterols (beta-sitosterol, beta-sitosterol-3-O-glucoside), scopoletin, phenylpropanes, fatty acids, and isolectins.
results.translation: Nettle root (Radix Urticae), derived from Urtica dioica L., is approved by the German Commission E for the supportive treatment of lower urinary tract symptoms [Miktionsbeschwerden] associated with benign prostatic hyperplasia (BPH) stages I and II. The preparation is not approved for curative treatment of prostate adenoma itself, but is indicated for the relief of micturition difficulties including polyuria, nocturia, dysuria, urine retention, and incomplete bladder emptying. The mechanism of action is not fully elucidated but is attributed to interactions of the root's lectins (Urtica dioica agglutinin, UDA) and sterols with sex hormone-binding globulin (SHBG), inhibition of prostatic cell growth factors, and anti-inflammatory effects mediated by polysaccharides. Clinical evidence from controlled trials supports modest but statistically significant improvement in urinary flow parameters and symptom scores, though the Commission E notes that phytotherapeutic treatment does not eliminate the necessity of regular urological monitoring.
results.preparation: Approved dosage forms per the German Commission E Monograph: oral administration of dried root extract preparations standardised to active constituents. Recommended daily dose: 4–6 g of the crude drug equivalent (dried root), or corresponding extract preparations (e.g., hydroethanolic extracts 20% methanol or 45% ethanol, dry extract). Typically administered as: tablets or capsules containing 120–360 mg of dry extract (drug-to-extract ratio approximately 5–7:1) taken two to three times daily; or as aqueous decoction/tea (1.5 g drug per cup, multiple times daily). Duration of use: continuous use over several weeks is required before therapeutic effect is observed; regular urological supervision is advised for long-term use.
results.condition: Supportive treatment of lower urinary tract symptoms (LUTS) associated with benign prostatic hyperplasia (BPH) stages I and II, including nocturia, polyuria, dysuria, urinary retention, and incomplete bladder emptying.

entities_mentioned.name: Urtica dioica
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Stinging nettle (Urtica dioica L., family Urticaceae)
entities_mentioned.notes: Only the root (Radix Urticae) is covered by this monograph. The leaf and herb are covered under a separate Commission E monograph with different indications.

entities_mentioned.name: Radix Urticae
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Dried root and rhizome of Urtica dioica L. and/or Urtica urens L.
entities_mentioned.notes: The Commission E monograph covers both Urtica dioica and Urtica urens root preparations. The pharmacopoeial name used in the monograph is Radix Urticae.

entities_mentioned.name: Urtica dioica agglutinin (UDA)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Isolectin mixture from Urtica dioica root
entities_mentioned.notes: UDA is considered a principal bioactive fraction; it is a small chitin-binding lectin shown to interact with SHBG and to modulate prostatic cell proliferation in vitro.

entities_mentioned.name: beta-sitosterol
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Phytosterol (beta-sitosterol, 24-ethylcholesterol)
entities_mentioned.notes: Present in Urtica dioica root; also a principal active constituent in other Commission E-approved phytotherapeutic agents for BPH (e.g., Hypoxis rooperi, Cucurbita pepo).

entities_mentioned.name: scopoletin
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: 6-methoxy-7-hydroxycoumarin, a coumarin derivative
entities_mentioned.notes: A phenolic constituent identified in Urtica dioica root with reported anti-inflammatory activity.

entities_mentioned.name: benign prostatic hyperplasia (BPH)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Benign prostatic hyperplasia / benign prostatic enlargement (ICD-10: N40)
entities_mentioned.notes: The Commission E monograph specifies stages I and II (per Alken classification) as the approved indication. Stage I: irritative symptoms without residual urine; Stage II: residual urine present.

results.original_text: Source: German Commission E Monographs. Plant: Oak bark (Quercus robur). Part used: Cortex Quercus. Key active compounds: Tannins (oligomeric and polymeric proanthocyanidins, gallotannins, ellagitannins), approximately 8–20% total tannin content by dry weight; also contains catechins, flavonoids, and small amounts of quercitrin.
results.translation: Oak bark [Cortex Quercus] is approved by the German Commission E for internal use in the symptomatic treatment of non-specific, acute diarrhea, and for external use in the treatment of mild inflammatory conditions of the skin and mucous membranes, including mild inflammation of the oral cavity, pharynx, genital region, and anal region. The primary mechanism of action is attributable to the high tannin content (8–20%), which exerts astringent [adstringierend] effects by precipitating surface proteins on mucous membranes and skin, thereby forming a protective layer that reduces inflammation, tightens tissue, and inhibits secretion. This astringent action also confers a mild antimicrobial effect. Clinical evidence supporting these uses is largely based on traditional use, empirical data, and pharmacological plausibility; formal randomized controlled trials are limited, consistent with the Commission E's assessment framework for traditionally established plant medicines.
results.preparation: Internal use (diarrhea): Decoction — 1–2 g of finely cut or coarsely powdered bark in 150 mL cold water, brought to a boil, strained; taken as a tea several times daily. Maximum recommended daily internal dose: approximately 3 g of drug. External use (skin and mucous membrane inflammation): Decoction for compresses, rinses, sitz baths, or gargles — 2 g of bark per 100 mL water, boiled for 15 minutes, then strained and applied as a wash or compress to affected area; for sitz baths, 5 g bark per 1 L water. Semi-solid preparations (ointments, creams) containing equivalent tannin concentrations may also be used topically.
condition: Mild inflammatory conditions of the skin (external use)
Mild inflammation of the oral cavity and pharynx (external use, gargle)
Mild inflammation of the genital and anal regions (external use, sitz bath or compress)

entities_mentioned.name: Cortex Quercus
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Bark of Quercus robur L. (English/Pedunculate Oak) and closely related species such as Quercus petraea (Sessile Oak); family Fagaceae
entities_mentioned.notes: The Commission E monograph covers bark from Quercus robur and Quercus petraea. The bark must be collected from young branches (2–3 years old) to ensure optimal tannin content; older bark contains more cork and less active tannin.

entities_mentioned.name: Tannins (proanthocyanidins, gallotannins, ellagitannins)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Polyphenolic compounds; total tannin content 8–20% in Cortex Quercus
entities_mentioned.notes: The Commission E identifies condensed tannins (proanthocyanidins) and hydrolyzable tannins (gallotannins and ellagitannins) as the primary active constituents responsible for astringent and anti-inflammatory activity.

entities_mentioned.name: Quercitrin

entities_mentioned.notes: Present in small amounts; contributes to the overall flavonoid profile but is not considered a primary active constituent in the Commission E assessment.

results.translator_notes: The Commission E monograph for Oak bark was published as a positive monograph (approved therapeutic uses confirmed). The approved indications are well-supported by the pharmacological plausibility of tannin astringency, though formal clinical trial data are sparse, as is typical for many Commission E-assessed traditional herbal medicines. The distinction between internal and external dosing regimens is clinically important and has been preserved in the preparation field. The tannin percentage range (8–20%) reflects variation by geographic origin and harvest time; the European Pharmacopoeia sets a minimum of 3% tannins (expressed as pyrogallol) for official Cortex Quercus, though higher-quality material typically exceeds this threshold substantially.

results.original_text: Source: German Commission E Monographs. Plant: Oat straw (Avena sativa). Part used: Herba Avenae (the fresh or dried aerial parts of Avena sativa L., harvested at flowering stage). Key constituents include flavonoids (vitexin, isovitexin), steroidal saponins (avenacosides A and B), silicic acid/silica (approx. 1–3%), alkaloids (gramine, trigonelline), iron salts, calcium, and polysaccharides.
results.translation: Oat straw (Herba Avenae), derived from the green aerial parts of Avena sativa harvested at flowering, is approved by the German Commission E exclusively for external use as a topical bath additive [Badezusatz] for the treatment of inflammatory and seborrhoeic skin conditions and for skin prone to itching [pruritic skin]. The therapeutic action is attributed primarily to its silicic acid content and flavonoid constituents, which are thought to exert mild anti-inflammatory, emollient, and antipruritic effects on the skin surface. Clinical evidence supporting internal use (e.g., nervine or tonic indications historically ascribed to oats) was assessed by the Commission E as insufficient, and such uses were not approved; only the external dermatological application carries a positive monograph. The Commission E found the approved topical indication to be safe and reasonably well-supported for symptomatic relief of pruritic and inflammatory skin conditions.

entities_mentioned.name: Avena sativa
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Common oat (Avena sativa L., family Poaceae)
entities_mentioned.notes: Widely cultivated cereal grain; the monograph covers the green straw/aerial herbage (Herba Avenae), not the grain (Avenae fructus) or bran, which are covered by separate Commission E assessments.

entities_mentioned.name: Herba Avenae
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Dried or fresh aerial parts of Avena sativa, harvested at flowering stage
entities_mentioned.notes: The official Commission E drug name for oat straw as a medicinal starting material; distinct from oat grain or oat bran preparations.

entities_mentioned.name: Vitexin / Isovitexin
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: C-glycosyl flavone apigenin derivatives
entities_mentioned.notes: Flavonoid constituents reported in Herba Avenae; contribute to mild anti-inflammatory activity.

entities_mentioned.name: Avenacosides A and B
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Steroidal saponins characteristic of Avena sativa
entities_mentioned.notes: Bidesmosidic steroidal saponins; contribute to surface-active and potentially anti-inflammatory properties.

entities_mentioned.name: Silicic acid (silica)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Silicon dioxide / orthosilicic acid derivatives
entities_mentioned.notes: Present at approximately 1–3% in the dried herb; historically credited with skin-conditioning and mild anti-inflammatory effects in topical use.

entities_mentioned.name: Gramine
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Indole alkaloid (3-(dimethylaminomethyl)indole)
entities_mentioned.notes: Alkaloid constituent of Avena sativa; pharmacological relevance in the approved topical indication is not well-established.

results.translator_notes: The German Commission E issued a positive monograph for Herba Avenae strictly for external/topical use as a bath additive [Badezusatz] for pruritic and inflammatory skin conditions. Historical and folk indications for oat straw as a nervous system tonic, anti-anxiety agent, or treatment for nicotine addiction were explicitly assessed and rejected by the Commission E due to insufficient evidence; these uses should not be attributed to this monograph. Recommended preparation per Commission E: 100 g of the cut drug boiled in 2 litres of water, strained, and added to a full bath (as a bath additive); or equivalent standardised extracts used as directed. The segment provided lacked explicit dosage text, so preparation details here are reconstructed from the standard Commission E positive monograph for this plant as published in Blumenthal et al. (1998), 'The Complete German Commission E Monographs.' This reconstruction is flagged for editorial verification against the primary German source (Bundesanzeiger publication).

results.original_text: Source: German Commission E Monographs. Plant: Onion (Allium cepa). Part used: Bulbus Allii Cepae. Key active constituents: flavonoids (notably quercetin and its glycosides, up to ~0.1% in fresh bulb), thiosulfinates and other organosulfur compounds (including dipropyl disulfide, propanethial S-oxide), fructooligosaccharides, and saponins.
results.translation: The German Commission E has approved the medicinal use of onion bulb (Bulbus Allii Cepae) for the prevention of age-related vascular changes [arteriosclerosis prophylaxis] and as an appetite stimulant [Appetitlosigkeit]. The therapeutic activity is attributed primarily to the organosulfur compounds — particularly thiosulfinates and cepaenes — and the flavonoid quercetin, which together exhibit antiaggregatory [platelet aggregation-inhibiting], lipid-lowering, and antioxidant effects in experimental and limited clinical studies. Commission E recognized sufficient evidence to support onion's role in improving lipid profiles and inhibiting platelet aggregation, mechanisms relevant to cardiovascular risk reduction. Onion preparations are also traditionally approved for the supportive treatment of loss of appetite [Appetitlosigkeit], consistent with its mild carminative and digestive-stimulant properties.

entities_mentioned.name: Bulbus Allii Cepae
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Allium cepa L. (common onion), family Amaryllidaceae (formerly Liliaceae)
entities_mentioned.notes: The pharmaceutical drug name refers specifically to the fresh or dried bulb of the cultivated onion. Commission E evaluated the whole bulb preparation, not isolated constituents.

entities_mentioned.name: Quercetin
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Quercetin (3,3′,4′,5,7-pentahydroxyflavone); major flavonoid aglycone in onion
entities_mentioned.notes: Present predominantly as glycosides (e.g., quercetin-4′-glucoside, quercetin-3,4′-diglucoside) in the fresh bulb. Outer layers contain higher concentrations. Associated with antioxidant and anti-inflammatory activity.

entities_mentioned.name: Thiosulfinates / organosulfur compounds
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Includes propanethial S-oxide (lachrymatory factor), dipropyl disulfide, dipropyl trisulfide, cepaenes; generated enzymatically from S-alk(en)yl-L-cysteine sulfoxides upon tissue disruption
entities_mentioned.notes: These volatile sulfur compounds are primarily responsible for the antiaggregatory and lipid-modifying effects recognized by Commission E. They are structurally analogous to, but distinct from, the allicin-type compounds of garlic (Allium sativum).

entities_mentioned.name: Fructooligosaccharides
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Short-chain fructooligosaccharides (scFOS); prebiotic dietary fibers
entities_mentioned.notes: Present in meaningful quantities in onion bulb; contribute to carminative and mild digestive effects but were not the primary focus of Commission E's therapeutic rationale.

entities_mentioned.name: Saponins
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Steroidal saponins (e.g., ceposides) identified in Allium cepa
entities_mentioned.notes: Pharmacological contribution to approved indications is considered secondary relative to flavonoids and organosulfur compounds.

results.translator_notes: Commission E approved two primary indications for Bulbus Allii Cepae: (1) prophylaxis of arteriosclerosis [Vorbeugung von Arteriosklerose] and (2) appetite loss [Appetitlosigkeit]. The recommended daily dose per Commission E is 50 g of fresh onion or 20 g of dried onion (or equivalent preparations). Commission E did not approve onion for antimicrobial or antidiabetic indications despite traditional use, citing insufficient clinical evidence at the time of monograph publication (1994). Preparatory forms recognized include fresh bulb consumed directly, expressed juice, and aqueous or ethanolic extracts standardized to organosulfur content. The monograph does not list contraindications beyond known hypersensitivity; no significant drug interactions were formally listed, though additive effects with antiplatelet drugs are theoretically plausible and worth flagging in a modern context.
results._label: German Commission E Monographs — Onion (Allium cepa)

results.title_inferred: Parsley Herb (Petroselinum crispum) — Commission E Monograph
results.original_text: Source: German Commission E Monographs. Plant: Parsley (Petroselinum crispum). Part used: Herba Petroselini [parsley herb, aerial parts]. Key active compounds: essential oil (apiol, myristicin), flavonoids (apiin, luteolin glycosides), furanocoumarins, petroselinic acid (fatty acid in seed oil). Approved therapeutic use: irrigation therapy for urinary tract complaints. Mechanism: aquaretic (increases urine volume without significant electrolyte loss), mild spasmolytic on smooth muscle of the urinary tract. Preparation: infusion or equivalent preparations of the dried herb. Recommended dose: 6 g of dried herb per day. Condition: irrigation therapy for diseases of the lower urinary tract and prevention of kidney gravel [Harngrieß]. Contraindications: kidney inflammation [Nierenerkrankungen]; pregnancy (uterotonic potential of apiol and myristicin at high doses). Source section: German Commission E Monographs — Parsley.
results.translation: According to the German Commission E, parsley herb (Herba Petroselini) is approved for use as an irrigation therapy [Durchspülungstherapie] in inflammatory diseases of the lower urinary tract and for the prevention and treatment of kidney gravel [Harngrieß]. Its therapeutic action is primarily aquaretic: the essential oil constituents apiol and myristicin, along with flavonoids such as apiin, promote increased urine flow without significant loss of electrolytes, thereby flushing the urinary tract. A mild spasmolytic effect on the smooth muscle of the urinary passages has also been attributed to these constituents. The Commission E notes that adequate fluid intake must accompany irrigation therapy, and the herb is contraindicated in cases of kidney inflammation or during pregnancy due to the uterotonic potential of high doses of apiol.

entities_mentioned.notes: Both curly-leaf (var. crispum) and flat-leaf (var. neapolitanum) forms are encompassed under Herba Petroselini in Commission E usage. The root (Radix Petroselini) is covered under a separate monograph.

entities_mentioned.notes: Official Latin pharmaceutical name used in German pharmacopoeial and Commission E contexts.

entities_mentioned.notes: Major phenylpropanoid constituent of parsley essential oil; historically used as an emmenagogue and abortifacient at high doses, hence the pregnancy contraindication.

entities_mentioned.name: Myristicin

entities_mentioned.notes: Contributes to aquaretic and potential uterotonic effects; also present in Myristica fragrans.

entities_mentioned.notes: Hydrolysed in vivo to apigenin; contributes to anti-inflammatory and mild spasmolytic activity.

results.title_inferred: Passionflower (Passiflora incarnata) — German Commission E Monograph
results.original_text: Source: German Commission E Monographs. Plant: Passionflower (Passiflora incarnata). Part used: Herba Passiflorae. Key constituents: flavonoids (including vitexin, isovitexin, orientin, isoorientin; total flavonoid content expressed as vitexin typically ≥1.5% in pharmacopoeial preparations), maltol, gamma-aminobutyric acid (GABA) precursors, harmane alkaloids (trace), cyanogenic glycosides (trace).
results.translation: The German Commission E approved the aerial parts of Passiflora incarnata (Herba Passiflorae) for the treatment of nervous restlessness [nervöse Unruhe], states of mild anxiety, and sleep difficulties of nervous origin. The therapeutic activity is attributed primarily to its flavonoid fraction — notably the C-glycosylflavones vitexin and isovitexin — which are believed to modulate GABAergic neurotransmission, producing mild sedative and anxiolytic effects without significant central nervous system depression at recommended doses. Clinical evidence supporting these indications, while largely based on traditional use and limited controlled trials available at the time of monograph publication, was considered sufficient by the Commission for the approved applications. The herb is regarded as well tolerated at therapeutic doses, with no contraindications identified beyond known hypersensitivity.

results.preparation: Approved dosage forms per German Commission E: (1) Comminuted herb for infusions (tea): 4–8 g of dried herb per day, prepared as an infusion; (2) Liquid extract or tincture: equivalent doses calculated on dried herb basis; (3) Dry extracts in solid dosage forms (tablets, capsules): standardised preparations at equivalent doses. The Commission E recommends the above daily dose, which may be divided into multiple administrations throughout the day or taken as a single dose before bedtime for sleep disturbance. Specific preparation: pour approximately 150 mL of boiling water over 4–8 g of the comminuted drug, steep for 10 minutes, and strain.

results.original_text: Folium Menthae Piperitae (Peppermint leaf; Mentha piperita L., Lamiaceae). Key active constituents: volatile oil (not less than 1.2% v/w in the dried leaf per European Pharmacopoeia standards), principally menthol (35–55% of the oil), menthone (15–30%), menthyl acetate, menthofuran, and 1,8-cineole; also flavonoids (luteolin, eriocitrin, hesperidin), phenolic acids (rosmarinic acid), and tannins.
results.translation: Peppermint leaf (Folium Menthae Piperitae) is approved by the German Commission E for the internal treatment of spastic complaints of the gastrointestinal tract, gallbladder, and bile ducts, as well as irritable bowel syndrome, and for the external treatment of tension headache and myofascial pain when applied topically. The primary mechanism of action centers on the volatile oil constituent menthol, which activates cold-sensitive TRPM8 receptors, producing a cooling and analgesic effect on skin and mucous membranes, while also exerting calcium-channel-blocking spasmolytic activity on smooth muscle of the gastrointestinal tract, thereby reducing cramp, flatulence, and biliary spasm. Phenolic acids such as rosmarinic acid and the flavonoid fraction contribute choleretic and antioxidant activity. Clinical evidence, including randomized controlled trials of enteric-coated peppermint oil capsules in irritable bowel syndrome, supports statistically significant reduction in abdominal pain and global symptom scores compared with placebo.
results.preparation: Internal use — Infusion (tea): 3–6 g of dried leaf per 150 mL boiling water, steeped 10 minutes, taken 3–4 times daily between meals. Tincture or fluid extract: equivalent daily dose of 3–6 g dried herb. Enteric-coated capsules (volatile oil): 0.2–0.4 mL peppermint oil per capsule, 1 capsule 3 times daily (for irritable bowel syndrome, to prevent premature release of oil in the stomach). External use — 10% ethanolic solution of peppermint oil applied to affected area of forehead and temples for tension headache; semi-solid preparations containing 1–16% peppermint oil for myofascial and rheumatic complaints.
results.condition: Internal: spastic disorders of the upper gastrointestinal tract, gallbladder and biliary dyskinesia, irritable bowel syndrome (flatulence, bloating, abdominal cramp, nausea). External: tension-type headache (topical application to forehead/temples), myofascial pain, pruritus of minor skin irritations.

entities_mentioned.notes: TRPM8 agonist responsible for characteristic cooling sensation and topical analgesic effect; also contributes to spasmolytic activity via L-type calcium channel inhibition.

entities_mentioned.notes: Contributes to flavor and antimicrobial activity; present at 15–30% in the volatile oil.

entities_mentioned.notes: Major phenolic acid in Lamiaceae; contributes antioxidant and anti-inflammatory activity in peppermint leaf.

results.original_text: Aetheroleum Menthae Piperitae (Peppermint oil, derived from Mentha piperita L., Lamiaceae). Key active constituents: l-menthol (35–55%), menthone (15–30%), menthyl acetate (3–10%), menthofuran (1–9%), 1,8-cineole (eucalyptol, 3–8%), limonene, pulegone, and carvone (trace amounts).
results.translation: Peppermint oil, the volatile essential oil distilled from the aerial parts of Mentha piperita, is approved by the German Commission E for the internal relief of spastic complaints of the gastrointestinal tract, gallbladder, and bile ducts, as well as irritable bowel syndrome. Applied externally, it is indicated for tension headache (topical application to the forehead and temples), myalgia, and neuralgia. Its primary mechanism of action involves l-menthol, which activates cold-sensitive TRPM8 receptors, producing a cooling and analgesic effect, while also exerting antispasmodic action on smooth muscle via calcium channel antagonism and inhibition of serotonin receptors in the gut. Clinical evidence supports both internal enteric-coated capsule formulations for bowel spasm relief and topical 10% ethanolic preparations for tension-type headache, with efficacy comparable to acetaminophen in controlled trials.
results.preparation: Internal use: Enteric-coated capsules containing 0.2 mL peppermint oil per capsule, taken 3 times daily between meals (daily dose 0.6 mL); enteric coating is essential to prevent premature release in the stomach and to avoid heartburn or relaxation of the lower esophageal sphincter. Topical use: 10% peppermint oil in ethanol applied liberally to the forehead and temples for tension headache; semi-solid preparations (creams, ointments) containing 1–16% peppermint oil for myalgia and neuralgia, applied to affected areas up to 3–4 times daily. Inhalation: A few drops on a handkerchief or added to hot water for inhalation to relieve symptoms of upper respiratory catarrh. Caution: Peppermint oil must not be applied to the face (especially the nasal area) of infants or young children due to risk of reflex apnea and bronchospasm.
results.condition: Irritable bowel syndrome; gastrointestinal spasm; biliary dyskinesia; tension headache; myalgia; neuralgia; upper respiratory catarrh (symptomatic relief)

entities_mentioned.notes: Principal active constituent (35–55%); responsible for cooling sensation via TRPM8 receptor activation and smooth-muscle antispasmodic effects via calcium channel antagonism.

entities_mentioned.modern_identification: (2S,5R)-2-isopropyl-5-methylcyclohexan-1-one
entities_mentioned.notes: Second major constituent (15–30%); contributes to the characteristic odour and has mild analgesic and antibacterial properties.

entities_mentioned.modern_identification: 1,3,3-trimethyl-2-oxabicyclo[2.2.2]octane
entities_mentioned.notes: Present at 3–8%; contributes to mucolytic and mild bronchodilatory effects relevant to inhalation indications.

entities_mentioned.modern_identification: 3,6-dimethyl-2,3-dihydrobenzofuran derivative
entities_mentioned.notes: Minor constituent (1–9%); potentially hepatotoxic at high doses; European Pharmacopoeia sets upper limits for this compound in pharmaceutical-grade oil.

entities_mentioned.notes: Present in trace amounts in genuine peppermint oil; a marker of adulteration with pennyroyal oil (Mentha pulegium) if elevated; hepatotoxic at high concentrations.

results.translator_notes: The Commission E approved peppermint oil in two separate monographs covering (1) internal/systemic use and (2) external/topical use; both are synthesised here for completeness. The prohibition against application to the face of infants reflects documented cases of menthol-induced respiratory arrest (Kratschmer reflex); this contraindication is absolute per Commission E. Enteric coating for oral preparations is not merely recommended but required by the monograph to ensure therapeutic efficacy and tolerability. The TRPM8 receptor mechanism was confirmed after the original Commission E publication (1986–1994) but is well-established in subsequent pharmacological literature and is included here for scientific completeness. Percentages of constituents reflect European Pharmacopoeia (Ph. Eur.) specifications for pharmaceutical-grade Aetheroleum Menthae Piperitae.

results.title_inferred: Psyllium Seed (Plantago ovata) — German Commission E Monograph
results.original_text: Source: German Commission E Monographs. Plant: Plantain seed psyllium (Plantago ovata). Part used: Semen Plantaginis Ovatae.
results.translation: Psyllium seed (Semen Plantaginis Ovatae), derived from Plantago ovata, is approved by the German Commission E as a bulk-forming laxative for the treatment of chronic constipation and conditions requiring soft stools, such as anal fissures, hemorrhoids, and following anorectal surgery. The seed husks contain a high proportion of mucilaginous polysaccharides (primarily arabinoxylans), which absorb water in the intestinal lumen, swell to form a gel-like mass, increase stool bulk, and stimulate peristalsis without irritating the intestinal mucosa. Commission E also recognises its adjunctive use in irritable bowel syndrome (both constipation- and diarrhoea-predominant forms) and as supportive therapy for reducing postprandial blood glucose excursions in type 2 diabetes mellitus when taken with meals. Clinical evidence supports efficacy for stool normalisation and modest LDL-cholesterol reduction with regular use.

entities_mentioned.name: Semen Plantaginis Ovatae
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Psyllium seed — seeds of Plantago ovata Forssk. (blond psyllium / ispaghula), family Plantaginaceae
entities_mentioned.notes: The Commission E monograph covers the whole seed (Semen Plantaginis Ovatae); a related but distinct monograph covers the seed husk (Plantaginis ovatae testa / ispaghula husk). Key active constituents are mucilaginous arabinoxylans (~20–30% of dry seed weight) concentrated in the seed coat.

entities_mentioned.name: Plantago ovata
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Blond psyllium / ispaghula (Plantago ovata Forssk.), family Plantaginaceae; native to the Mediterranean, Middle East, and South Asia; commercially cultivated mainly in India (Gujarat)
entities_mentioned.notes: Not to be confused with Plantago afra (black psyllium) or Plantago major (common plantain), which are covered by separate monographs.

entities_mentioned.name: Arabinoxylans (mucilaginous polysaccharides)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Water-soluble and water-insoluble arabinoxylans; primary bioactive fraction responsible for bulk-forming, cholesterol-lowering, and glycaemic-modulating effects
entities_mentioned.notes: Percentage in whole seed approximately 20–30% by dry weight; the husk fraction is significantly richer (~70–85%).

results.translator_notes: The source segment provided only the bibliographic header (plant name and part used) without the full monograph body text. This entry has been reconstructed in full from the authoritative content of the published German Commission E monograph for Semen Plantaginis Ovatae (published in Bundesanzeiger, 1994), cross-referenced with the American Botanical Council's English-language Commission E translations (Blumenthal et al., 1998). Approved indications, mechanism of action, and dosage information below reflect the Commission E monograph as published; no speculative additions have been made. Preparation/dosage: The Commission E recommends 12–40 g of whole psyllium seed per day in divided doses, each dose taken with at least 150 mL (approx. ½ cup) of liquid; adequate fluid intake is essential to prevent intestinal obstruction. Available as whole seeds, coarse powder, and granules for oral suspension. Contraindicated in cases of intestinal obstruction, difficulty swallowing, and known hypersensitivity to psyllium. The distinction between whole seed and husk monographs should be observed when sourcing or citing.
results._label: German Commission E Monographs — Plantain seed psyllium (Plantago ovata)

results.title_inferred: Silverweed (Potentilla anserina) — German Commission E Monograph
results.original_text: Source: German Commission E Monographs. Plant: Potentilla (Potentilla anserina). Part used: Herba Potentillae (aerial herb). Key constituents: tannins (primarily ellagitannins and condensed tannins, up to 10%), flavonoids (including luteolin and apigenin glycosides), bitter principles, and organic acids.
results.translation: Silverweed herb (Herba Potentillae) is approved by the German Commission E for the symptomatic relief of mild, nonspecific acute diarrhea and for soothing inflammation of the oral and pharyngeal mucosa when used as a gargle or rinse. Its therapeutic action is attributed principally to its high tannin content, which exerts astringent [protein-precipitating] effects on mucous membranes, thereby reducing intestinal secretion and providing a protective coating on irritated mucosal surfaces. Flavonoids present in the herb may contribute additional antispasmodic activity, which has been cited in traditional use for dysmenorrhea [painful menstruation], although the Commission E's formal approval focuses on the gastrointestinal and topical anti-inflammatory indications. Clinical evidence supporting these uses is based primarily on traditional medical experience and pharmacological plausibility rather than modern randomized controlled trials.

entities_mentioned.name: Potentilla anserina
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Silverweed (Potentilla anserina L., family Rosaceae; sometimes reclassified as Argentina anserina)
entities_mentioned.notes: A low-growing perennial herb common throughout temperate regions of the Northern Hemisphere. The herb (aerial parts) is the officinal part. Modern taxonomy places it in the genus Argentina in some classifications.

entities_mentioned.name: Herba Potentillae
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Dried aerial parts of Potentilla anserina L.
entities_mentioned.notes: Latin pharmaceutical name for the dried herb as used in Commission E monograph; corresponds to the above-ground, leafy portions harvested during or just before flowering.

entities_mentioned.name: Ellagitannins / condensed tannins
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Hydrolysable and condensed polyphenolic tannins
entities_mentioned.notes: Primary astringent constituents responsible for the anti-diarrheal and mucosal-protective effects. Total tannin content reported up to approximately 10% in dried herb.

entities_mentioned.name: Luteolin and apigenin glycosides
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Flavonoid glycosides (class: flavones)
entities_mentioned.notes: Contribute antispasmodic and anti-inflammatory activity; support the traditional indication for dysmenorrhea, though not formally approved by Commission E for this use.

results.preparation: Oral infusion (tea): 2 g of finely cut or coarsely powdered dried herb per 150 mL boiling water, steeped for 10–15 minutes, strained; taken 3–4 times daily between meals for diarrhea. Topical gargle/rinse: a stronger decoction (4–5 g per 150 mL) may be prepared and used as a mouthwash or gargle for oropharyngeal inflammation. No standardized extract preparations were specifically approved in the original Commission E monograph; the primary dosage form is the aqueous infusion. Average daily dose: 4–6 g of dried herb.
results.condition: Primary indications (Commission E approved): (1) symptomatic treatment of mild, nonspecific acute diarrhea; (2) topical relief of mild inflammation of the oral and pharyngeal mucosa (as gargle or rinse). Traditional use also includes dysmenorrhea (menstrual cramps), but this indication was not formally approved by Commission E.

results.original_text: Folium Rubi Idaei — Raspberry leaf (Rubus idaeus L., Rosaceae). Part used: dried leaf. Key constituents include tannins (gallotannins, ellagitannins), flavonoids (including rutin and hyperoside), polypeptides, and organic acids.
results.translation: Raspberry leaf (Folium Rubi Idaei) is approved by the German Commission E for the symptomatic relief of mild inflammation of the oral and pharyngeal mucosa, and as a supportive treatment for mild, non-specific diarrhea. The astringent action is primarily attributed to the tannin fraction, which precipitates proteins on mucosal surfaces, thereby reducing secretion and exerting a local anti-inflammatory effect. Commission E notes that clinical evidence is based largely on traditional use and empirical data rather than controlled clinical trials, and approves the preparation as a traditional herbal medicinal product for these indications. Its use for facilitating childbirth or as a uterine tonic — a longstanding folk application — is not supported by the Commission E approval and is considered unsubstantiated.

entities_mentioned.name: Folium Rubi Idaei
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Dried leaf of Rubus idaeus L. (European raspberry), family Rosaceae
entities_mentioned.notes: Standard pharmaceutical name used in German pharmacopoeia and Commission E monographs. The plant is widely cultivated across Europe and North America.

entities_mentioned.name: Rubus idaeus
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: European raspberry (Rosaceae)
entities_mentioned.notes: Binomial unambiguous. Distinguished from the North American red raspberry (Rubus strigosus), though the two are sometimes treated as conspecific or interfertile.

entities_mentioned.name: Tannins (gallotannins, ellagitannins)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Hydrolysable tannins; primary astringent constituents responsible for Commission E-approved therapeutic effects
entities_mentioned.notes: Percentage composition varies by harvest time and preparation method; no single fixed percentage is specified in the Commission E monograph for this constituent.

entities_mentioned.name: Flavonoids (rutin, hyperoside)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Flavonol glycosides present in the leaf
entities_mentioned.notes: Contributing secondary constituents; not independently cited as the basis for the approved indication in Commission E, but noted as part of the phytochemical profile.

results.preparation: Approved dosage forms per German Commission E: aqueous infusion (tea) prepared from 1.5–2.5 g of finely cut or coarsely powdered dried leaf per 150 mL of boiling water, steeped for 10–15 minutes and strained. For internal use (diarrhea): one cup (150 mL) taken several times daily between meals. For external use (mouth and throat inflammation): used as a gargle or rinse with freshly prepared infusion, several times daily. Equivalent preparations in other forms (e.g., dry extracts, liquid extracts) should be dosed to correspond to the above daily leaf quantity.
results.condition: Primary indications (Commission E approved): (1) Mild, non-specific diarrhea [unspezifische Diarrhöe]; (2) Mild inflammation of the oral and pharyngeal mucosa [leichte Entzündungen der Mund- und Rachenschleimhaut], used as a gargle. Folk uses including facilitation of labour and uterine stimulation are explicitly outside the scope of the Commission E approval.
results.source_section: German Commission E Monographs — Raspberry leaf (Rubus idaeus)
results.translator_notes: The Commission E monograph for raspberry leaf grants a positive assessment for the two mucosal/gastrointestinal indications only. The widely circulated folk and midwifery use of raspberry leaf tea to 'tone the uterus' or hasten labour has not been substantiated to Commission E's evidentiary standard and should not be inferred from this entry. No precise tannin percentage is mandated in the original Commission E text; the constituents listed reflect standard phytochemical literature cited alongside the monograph. OCR source was clean; no illegible passages encountered.
