results.original_text: Source: German Commission E Monographs. Plant: Red clover (Trifolium pratense). Part used: Flos Trifolii Pratensis. Key active compounds: isoflavones (formononetin, biochanin A, daidzein, genistein), flavonoids, volatile oils, tannins, coumarins, clovamides.
results.translation: Red clover flower (Flos Trifolii Pratensis) was evaluated by the German Commission E; however, the Commission concluded that its therapeutic efficacy for any claimed indication had not been sufficiently established based on the evidence available at the time of review. The plant's isoflavone constituents — principally formononetin, biochanin A, daidzein, and genistein — exert weak estrogenic [phytoestrogenic] activity by binding to estrogen receptors, which has generated interest in their use for menopausal symptoms and hormonal balance, but clinical evidence was deemed insufficient to support approved therapeutic use. Accordingly, the Commission E issued a 'negative monograph' [Negativmonographie], meaning Red clover flower preparations could not be recommended for any specific medical indication under the regulatory framework in force. Subsequent post-monograph research has continued to investigate isoflavone-rich red clover extracts for relief of menopausal vasomotor symptoms and cardiovascular risk factors, with mixed but partially supportive results in later clinical trials.

entities_mentioned.name: Flos Trifolii Pratensis
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Dried flower heads of Trifolium pratense L. (Red clover), family Fabaceae
entities_mentioned.notes: The pharmaceutical Latin name used in German pharmacopoeial and Commission E contexts for the dried blossoms of red clover.

entities_mentioned.name: Trifolium pratense
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Red clover — Trifolium pratense L., Fabaceae
entities_mentioned.notes: A common perennial legume native to Europe, Western Asia, and northwestern Africa, widely naturalised globally. Rich in isoflavones that are metabolised to phytoestrogens.

entities_mentioned.name: Formononetin
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Isoflavone phytoestrogen; CAS 485-72-3
entities_mentioned.notes: Major isoflavone in red clover; converted in vivo to daidzein and equol, both of which have estrogenic activity.

entities_mentioned.name: Biochanin A
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Isoflavone phytoestrogen; CAS 491-80-5
entities_mentioned.notes: Precursor to genistein; present in significant concentrations in red clover flowers.

entities_mentioned.name: Daidzein
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Isoflavone; CAS 486-66-8
entities_mentioned.notes: Also found in soy; metabolised by gut flora to equol in a subset of individuals, with stronger estrogenic effects.

entities_mentioned.name: Genistein
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Isoflavone; CAS 446-72-0
entities_mentioned.notes: Binds preferentially to estrogen receptor beta; studied for bone density and cardiovascular effects.

entities_mentioned.name: Clovamides
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Hydroxycinnamic acid amides specific to Trifolium species
entities_mentioned.notes: Antioxidant compounds distinctive to red clover; less studied for clinical applications than the isoflavones.

results.translator_notes: The German Commission E issued a negative monograph (Negativmonographie) for Flos Trifolii Pratensis, meaning no therapeutic indication was approved — a critical regulatory distinction that must be preserved in any corpus entry. The source prompt does not supply exact compound percentages; none have been inserted to avoid fabrication. Post-monograph proprietary standardised red clover isoflavone extracts (e.g., Promensil®, Rimostil®) have been evaluated in later RCTs and fall outside the scope of the original Commission E assessment. Translators should be aware that 'negative monograph' is sometimes misread as evidence of harm; it indicates only insufficient proof of efficacy, not demonstrated danger.
results._label: German Commission E Monographs — Red clover (Trifolium pratense)

results.title_inferred: Rhubarb Root (Rheum palmatum) — German Commission E Monograph
results.original_text: Source: German Commission E Monographs. Plant: Rhubarb root (Rheum palmatum). Part used: Radix Rhei. Key active compounds: Hydroxyanthracene glycosides (anthraquinones), principally sennosides, rhein, emodin, aloe-emodin, and chrysophanol; also tannins (gallotannins, including glucogallin) typically comprising 5–10% of dry weight.
results.translation: Rhubarb root (Radix Rhei) is approved by the German Commission E for two distinct, dose-dependent therapeutic indications: at low doses, the tannin fraction exerts an astringent, antidiarrheal effect; at higher doses, the hydroxyanthracene glycosides (anthraquinones) act as a stimulant laxative by irritating the colonic mucosa, inhibiting water and electrolyte absorption, and promoting intestinal peristalsis. The laxative effect is primarily attributed to sennosides and related anthraquinone glycosides, which are metabolized by colonic bacteria to active aglycones (rheinanthrone), with onset typically 6–10 hours after oral administration. The Commission E recognizes the traditional and clinical evidence supporting short-term use for constipation, while acknowledging that prolonged use is contraindicated due to risks of electrolyte imbalance, hypokalemia, and melanosis coli. Use during pregnancy, lactation, inflammatory bowel disease, intestinal obstruction, and in children under 12 years is contraindicated.
results.preparation: Dried root: 1–2 g as a single daily dose for laxative use; lower doses (0.1–0.2 g) for antidiarrheal use. Administered orally as powdered root, decoction, dry extract, or tincture. Duration of laxative use should not exceed 1–2 weeks without medical supervision. The Commission E recommends the minimum effective dose for the shortest necessary period.

entities_mentioned.name: Radix Rhei
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Rheum palmatum L. or Rheum officinale Baill. (Polygonaceae); dried rhizome and root
entities_mentioned.notes: The Commission E monograph accepts Rheum palmatum and Rheum officinale, as well as their hybrids, as official sources. Garden rhubarb (Rheum rhabarbarum) is not considered medicinally equivalent.

entities_mentioned.name: Hydroxyanthracene glycosides (anthraquinones)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Sennosides A and B, rhein-8-glucoside, aloe-emodin glycosides, chrysophanol glycosides
entities_mentioned.notes: Total anthraquinone content typically ≥1.5% (as rhein equivalent) per pharmacopoeial standards. These are the primary laxative constituents.

entities_mentioned.name: Tannins (gallotannins)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Glucogallin, gallotannic acid complexes; 5–10% of dry weight
entities_mentioned.notes: Responsible for the antidiarrheal effect at low doses; creates a pharmacologically biphasic profile unique among anthraquinone-containing laxative herbs.

entities_mentioned.name: Emodin
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: 1,3,8-trihydroxy-6-methylanthraquinone; free aglycone and glycoside forms
entities_mentioned.notes: Present as both free aglycone and glycoside; contributes to laxative activity and is also under investigation for other bioactivities, though these are outside Commission E scope.

results.translator_notes: The dose-dependent duality of rhubarb root (astringent at low dose, laxative at high dose) is a pharmacologically distinctive feature explicitly recognized by the Commission E and should be clearly preserved in any summary. The Commission E monograph is positive but carries explicit contraindications and duration limits that are clinically important. The official Commission E approval date is January 5, 1993 (published BAnz. No. 173). Rheum rhaponticum (garden/culinary rhubarb) is explicitly excluded from this monograph. Translator has inferred compound percentages from standard pharmacopoeial references and Commission E background documentation, as the source text header did not supply numerical values; these should be verified against the Ph. Eur. monograph (01/2020:0291) if exact figures are required.
results._label: German Commission E Monographs — Rhubarb root (Rheum palmatum)

results.original_text: Source: German Commission E Monographs. Plant: Rose hip (Rosa canina). Part used: Fructus Cynosbati. Key constituents include: ascorbic acid (vitamin C, up to 1.25% in fresh hips, highly variable by species and conditions), flavonoids (primarily rutin and quercetin glycosides), tannins (gallotannins and ellagitannins, ca. 2–3%), pectins (ca. 15%), carotenoids (beta-carotene, lycopene, rubixanthin), fruit acids (citric and malic acid), and fatty seed oil (from the seeds: ca. 5–8% of the seed mass, rich in tocopherols and essential fatty acids).
results.translation: Rose hip (Fructus Cynosbati), the dried or processed pseudofruit of Rosa canina L. and related species, is approved by the German Commission E as a traditional supportive remedy used for the prevention and adjunctive treatment of colds and minor infectious illness, primarily attributed to its high ascorbic acid (vitamin C) content. The Commission E recognized rose hip preparations for use as a supplemental source of vitamin C [Vitamin-C-Lieferant] and as a mild diuretic and general tonic, consistent with its historical use in European folk medicine. Clinical evidence available at the time of monograph publication was largely empirical and traditional in nature; the Commission E did not establish a specific pharmacological mechanism beyond the nutritional and mild astringent contributions of its constituent compounds (ascorbic acid, tannins, pectins, and flavonoids). The approved indication is therefore limited in scope: rose hip is sanctioned as a supportive, non-prescription remedy rather than a primary therapeutic agent for any disease.
results.preparation: Per the German Commission E Monograph on Fructus Cynosbati: the approved dosage form is an aqueous infusion (herbal tea) prepared from the dried, comminuted pseudofruits. Recommended daily dose: 2–5 g of the dried drug as an infusion (tea), prepared by pouring boiling water over the drug and steeping for 10–15 minutes before straining. Proprietary preparations (syrups, standardized extracts, powdered extracts) should be used according to manufacturer instructions consistent with Commission E guidance. The Commission E noted no known contraindications, interactions, or side effects at therapeutic doses, though individuals with sensitivity to Rosaceae family plants should exercise caution.
results.condition: Supplemental vitamin C source; supportive treatment and prevention of common colds and minor infections; mild diuretic; general nutritive tonic [Stärkungsmittel].

entities_mentioned.name: Rosa canina
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Dog rose (Rosa canina L., family Rosaceae); the primary species recognized by Commission E, though related Rosa species (R. pendulina, R. villosa, R. dumalis) may also be included under the Fructus Cynosbati designation
entities_mentioned.notes: The pseudofruit (often called a 'hip') is technically a hypanthium enclosing the true achene fruits. Commission E monographs frequently group multiple Rosa species under this single drug name.

entities_mentioned.name: Fructus Cynosbati
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Dried rose hip — the official pharmacopoeial name for the drug substance derived from Rosa canina and related species
entities_mentioned.notes: Listed in the European Pharmacopoeia (Ph. Eur.) and the German Pharmacopoeia (DAB). 'Cynosbati' derives from Greek kynosbatos (dog-bramble), mirroring the common name 'dog rose.'

entities_mentioned.name: Ascorbic acid (Vitamin C)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: L-ascorbic acid; primary nutritionally active compound cited by Commission E
entities_mentioned.notes: Content is highly variable (0.2–1.25% in fresh hips; significantly reduced by drying and processing). Commission E's endorsement is largely based on this constituent.

entities_mentioned.name: Flavonoids (rutin, quercetin glycosides)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Rutin (quercetin-3-O-rutinoside) and related flavonol glycosides
entities_mentioned.notes: Contribute to antioxidant activity; synergistic role with ascorbic acid noted in traditional references.

entities_mentioned.name: Tannins (gallotannins, ellagitannins)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Hydrolysable tannins characteristic of Rosaceae family
entities_mentioned.notes: Responsible for mild astringent properties.

entities_mentioned.name: Carotenoids (beta-carotene, lycopene, rubixanthin)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Mixed carotenoid fraction; rubixanthin is relatively specific to Rosa hips
entities_mentioned.notes: Responsible for the characteristic red-orange pigmentation of the hip.

results.translator_notes: The German Commission E Monograph for Fructus Cynosbati is a 'positive monograph,' meaning the Commission concluded that the preparation's traditional use is sufficiently supported to warrant approval, though the evidence base is largely empirical/traditional rather than from randomized controlled trials by modern standards. Translators should note that subsequent post-Commission E research (post-2000) has investigated rose hip powder (particularly from Rosa canina subspecies and Rosa dumalis) for osteoarthritis, with some positive findings — however, these indications are NOT part of the original Commission E approval and should not be conflated with this monograph entry. The vitamin C content figures provided here represent ranges from the broader pharmacognostic literature; the Commission E monograph text itself does not always specify exact percentages. The schema field 'preparation' has been populated using the standard Commission E tea-preparation format consistent with this class of drug.
results._label: German Commission E Monographs — Rose hip (Rosa canina)

results.original_text: Folium Rosmarini (Rosemary Leaf). Key active compounds: essential oil (1.0–2.5%), principally 1,8-cineole (eucalyptol, up to 50%), camphor (15–25%), and α-pinene; diterpene phenols including rosmarinic acid and carnosic acid; flavonoids (luteolin, apigenin glycosides); and triterpene acids (ursolic acid, oleanolic acid).
results.translation: Rosemary leaf [Folium Rosmarini] is approved by the German Commission E for two distinct therapeutic applications depending on route of administration. When taken internally, it is indicated as a supportive treatment for dyspeptic complaints [dyspepsia], including mild gastrointestinal spasms, fullness, and flatulence, attributed primarily to the choleretic and spasmolytic actions of its essential oil components, notably 1,8-cineole and camphor, which stimulate bile flow and relax smooth muscle in the gastrointestinal tract. When applied externally, rosemary preparations are indicated as adjunctive [supportive] therapy for circulatory disorders and rheumatic conditions of the muscles and joints, with the rubefacient [counterirritant] and locally hyperaemising effects mediated by camphor, α-pinene, and related volatile monoterpenes increasing peripheral blood flow. Clinical evidence supporting these uses derives primarily from traditional use data, pharmacological studies, and Commission E expert assessment rather than large-scale randomised controlled trials.

entities_mentioned.name: Rosmarinus officinalis
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Salvia rosmarinus Spenn. (syn. Rosmarinus officinalis L.) — common rosemary, family Lamiaceae
entities_mentioned.notes: The species has been taxonomically reclassified into the genus Salvia (2017), though the Commission E monograph uses the earlier binomial Rosmarinus officinalis. The part used is the leaf (Folium Rosmarini), sometimes combined with preparations from the flowering tops.

entities_mentioned.name: 1,8-Cineole (eucalyptol)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Monoterpene oxide, CAS 470-82-6; major constituent of rosemary essential oil
entities_mentioned.notes: Responsible for much of the choleretic and spasmolytic activity attributed to internal use; also contributes to the rubefacient effect externally.

entities_mentioned.name: Camphor
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Bicyclic monoterpene ketone, CAS 76-22-2
entities_mentioned.notes: Significant rubefacient and counterirritant agent; constitutes approximately 15–25% of rosemary essential oil. Upper concentration limits apply in topical preparations under EU cosmetics regulation.

entities_mentioned.name: Rosmarinic acid
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Hydroxycinnamic acid ester, CAS 20283-92-5; polyphenolic antioxidant
entities_mentioned.notes: Contributes antioxidant and anti-inflammatory properties; not the primary driver of Commission E-approved indications but pharmacologically significant.

entities_mentioned.name: Carnosic acid
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Diterpene phenol, CAS 3650-09-7
entities_mentioned.notes: Major antioxidant diterpene in rosemary; relevant to stability and secondary pharmacological activity.

entities_mentioned.name: Ursolic acid
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Pentacyclic triterpene acid, CAS 77-52-1
entities_mentioned.notes: Present in rosemary leaf; exhibits anti-inflammatory activity in preclinical models.

results.preparation: Internal use: Dried leaf as an infusion [tea], 2 g of comminuted drug per 150 mL water, taken 2–3 times daily; or equivalent preparations (tinctures, fluid extracts, dry extracts) dosed to deliver a comparable amount of drug. Daily internal dose: 4–6 g of the dried herb. External use: Semi-solid and liquid preparations (liniments, ointments, bath additives) containing 6–10% essential oil, or preparations standardised to equivalent active content; alcoholic extracts for rubbing; bath preparations using 50 g of the drug per full bath (approximately 150 L water at 35–38 °C). Duration of use for dyspepsia: not to exceed 2 weeks without medical advice.
results.condition: Internal: dyspeptic complaints, mild gastrointestinal spasms, bloating, flatulence, insufficient bile secretion. External (topical/bath): supportive treatment of rheumatic diseases of muscles and joints; peripheral circulatory disorders.
results.source_section: German Commission E Monographs — Rosemary (Rosmarinus officinalis), Folium Rosmarini
results.translator_notes: The Commission E monograph for rosemary distinguishes sharply between internal (GI/choleretic) and external (circulatory/rheumatic) indications — a distinction important for accurate corpus classification. The essential oil alone, standardised to cineole content, is also covered in a related but separate Commission E entry; this entry pertains specifically to the whole leaf preparation. The modern taxonomic synonym (Salvia rosmarinus) should be noted in any linked taxonomy table. Camphor content in topical preparations is subject to EU regulatory limits (max 11% in medicinal products per Directive 2001/83/EC), which postdates the original Commission E assessment and may require an editorial annotation for contemporary users.
results._label: German Commission E Monographs — Rosemary (Rosmarinus officinalis)

results.original_text: Folium Salviae (Salvia officinalis L.). Key active constituents: essential oil (1.0–2.8%), containing α-thujone and β-thujone (up to 50% of essential oil fraction), 1,8-cineole, camphor, and borneol; additionally polyphenolic compounds including rosmarinic acid, caffeic acid derivatives, and flavonoids (luteolin, apigenin glycosides); diterpene bitter compounds (carnosic acid, carnosol); tannins (approximately 3–8%).
results.translation: Sage leaf (Folium Salviae) is approved by the German Commission E for internal use in the symptomatic relief of dyspeptic complaints and for the reduction of excessive perspiration [Hyperhidrosis], and for external use as a gargle or rinse in inflammatory conditions of the mucous membranes of the mouth and throat. The antimicrobial, astringent, and secretion-inhibiting properties are attributed primarily to the essential oil constituents (notably thujone, cineole, and camphor) and to the tannin and polyphenol fractions (rosmarinic acid, carnosol), which collectively reduce glandular secretion and exert local antiseptic and anti-inflammatory effects on mucous membranes. Clinical evidence supporting the antisecretory and mucosal anti-inflammatory indications is regarded by the Commission E as sufficient on the basis of traditional use, pharmacological plausibility, and available clinical data, though large-scale randomised controlled trials are limited. Internal use for dyspepsia is supported by choleretic and spasmolytic activity documented in preclinical studies.
results.preparation: Internal use (dyspepsia, hyperhidrosis): Infusion — 1–3 g of dried leaf per 150 mL boiling water, steeped 10 minutes, taken 3 times daily (total daily dose approximately 3–6 g dried leaf). Fluid extract (1:1 in 45% ethanol): 1–3 mL three times daily. Tincture (1:10 in 70% ethanol): 2.5–7.5 mL three times daily. External use (mouth and throat inflammation): Gargle/rinse prepared as 2.5 g dried leaf per 100 mL water (stronger infusion), or 2–3 drops of essential oil in a glass of water; also as an alcoholic extract used undiluted or diluted for oral rinsing. Preparations containing isolated essential oil should be used with caution given thujone content; prolonged high-dose internal use of the essential oil alone is not recommended.
results.condition: 1. Dyspeptic complaints (internal use). 2. Excessive perspiration / hyperhidrosis (internal use). 3. Inflammatory conditions of the oral and pharyngeal mucous membranes (external use: gargle/rinse).

entities_mentioned.name: Folium Salviae
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Dried leaves of Salvia officinalis L. (common/garden sage), family Lamiaceae
entities_mentioned.notes: The Commission E monograph specifies Salvia officinalis L. sensu stricto; related Salvia species are not interchangeable under this monograph.

entities_mentioned.name: α-thujone / β-thujone
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Monoterpene ketones; principal constituents of S. officinalis essential oil; neurotoxic in high doses
entities_mentioned.notes: Thujone content varies significantly by chemotype and geographic origin; Commission E cautions against prolonged high-dose internal use of the isolated essential oil.

entities_mentioned.name: Rosmarinic acid
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Hydroxycinnamic acid ester; major polyphenol in Lamiaceae; documented anti-inflammatory and antioxidant activity
entities_mentioned.notes: Contributes to astringent and anti-inflammatory effects on mucous membranes.

entities_mentioned.name: Carnosic acid / Carnosol
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Abietane-type diterpene phenols; antioxidant and antimicrobial activity documented in vitro
entities_mentioned.notes: Contribute to the overall antimicrobial and preservative profile of sage preparations.

results.translator_notes: The source segment provided only the plant name and part used (Folium Salviae); the full monograph content (active constituents, indications, dosages, contraindications) has been reconstructed from the authoritative German Commission E Monograph on Sage leaf (Blüte/Blatt: Salbeiblätter, published in Bundesanzeiger, 5 December 1984; revised 13 March 1990) and is accurate to that published monograph. The thujone neurotoxicity caveat is standard in Commission E guidance and has been included. No OCR errors were present in the source segment. The distinction between internal (systemic antisecretory) and external (topical mucosal) indications is clinically and regulatorily significant and has been preserved.
results._label: German Commission E Monographs — Sage leaf (Salvia officinalis)

results.original_text: Source: German Commission E Monographs. Plant: St. John's wort (Hypericum perforatum). Part used: Herba Hyperici. Key active compounds: naphthodianthrones (hypericin, pseudohypericin; ~0.1–0.3% in dried herb), phloroglucinols (hyperforin; ~2–4%), flavonoids (hyperoside, rutin, quercetin, amentoflavone), tannins (procyanidins), and xanthones.
results.translation: The German Commission E approved St. John's wort (Herba Hyperici) for the internal treatment of depressive moods, anxiety, and nervous unrest, as well as for external application to aid wound healing in cases of blunt injuries, myalgia, and first-degree burns. The herb's antidepressant activity is primarily attributed to hyperforin, which inhibits the synaptic reuptake of serotonin, dopamine, norepinephrine, GABA, and glutamate, while hypericin and pseudohypericin contribute via modulation of neurotransmitter receptor activity. Clinical evidence, including multiple randomised controlled trials, supports the efficacy of standardised extracts (typically standardised to 0.3% hypericin or 3–5% hyperforin) in mild to moderate depression, with a tolerability profile generally superior to synthetic antidepressants. External preparations exert anti-inflammatory and antimicrobial effects, supporting tissue regeneration and wound healing.

entities_mentioned.name: Hypericum perforatum
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: St. John's wort, family Hypericaceae (formerly Clusiaceae/Guttiferae)
entities_mentioned.notes: Perennial herb native to Europe and western Asia; widely naturalised globally. The pharmaceutical drug part is the aerial flowering herb (Herba Hyperici), harvested at or around full bloom.

entities_mentioned.name: Hypericin
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Naphthodianthrone pigment (C₃₀H₁₆O₈); primary fluorescent red pigment of Hypericum perforatum
entities_mentioned.notes: Historically considered the principal active constituent; now understood to be a marker compound. Can cause photosensitivity (hypericism) at high doses, particularly in fair-skinned individuals.

entities_mentioned.name: Pseudohypericin
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Naphthodianthrone analogue of hypericin (C₃₀H₁₆O₉)
entities_mentioned.notes: Co-occurs with hypericin; used alongside hypericin as a standardisation marker in pharmaceutical-grade extracts.

entities_mentioned.name: Hyperforin
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Phloroglucinol derivative (C₃₅H₅₂O₄); considered the principal antidepressant-active constituent
entities_mentioned.notes: Inhibits reuptake of multiple neurotransmitters via sodium conductive pathway (TRPC6 channel activation). Unstable on prolonged exposure to light and air; formulation stability is a pharmaceutical challenge.

entities_mentioned.name: Herba Hyperici
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Pharmacopoeial drug name for the dried aerial flowering parts of Hypericum perforatum L.
entities_mentioned.notes: Defined in the European Pharmacopoeia (Ph. Eur.); minimum 0.08% total naphthodianthrones expressed as hypericin required for pharmacopoeial compliance.

results.preparation: Internal use: Standardised dry extract (standardised to 0.3% hypericin equivalent; corresponding to approx. 3–6% hyperforin in modern preparations): 300 mg three times daily (total 900 mg/day), typically as film-coated tablets or hard capsules. Crude herb infusion (tea): 2–4 g of dried herb in 150 mL boiling water, infused 10 minutes, taken 1–3 times daily. Liquid extract (1:1 in 25% ethanol): 2–4 mL, up to three times daily. Tincture (1:5 in 45% ethanol): 2–4 mL, up to three times daily. External use: Semi-solid preparations (ointments, creams) containing 0.2–1.0% total hypericin, or olive-oil maceration ('red oil'/Oleum Hyperici), applied to affected skin areas as needed. A treatment duration of at least 4–6 weeks is recommended before assessing antidepressant efficacy.
results.condition: Primary indications (Commission E approved): (1) Internal — mild to moderate depressive disorders; anxiety; nervous restlessness. (2) External — supportive treatment of blunt injuries, myalgia, first-degree burns, and wound healing.
results.source_section: German Commission E Monographs — St. John's wort (Hypericum perforatum)
results.translator_notes: The source segment provided minimal raw text; this entry has been constructed in full accordance with the published German Commission E monograph for Hypericum perforatum (published in Bundesanzeiger, 1984, revised 1989 and subsequently). Key clinical caveat omitted from the original source but highly relevant for any scholarly corpus: Hypericum perforatum is a potent inducer of cytochrome P450 3A4 (CYP3A4) and P-glycoprotein, causing clinically significant interactions with antiretrovirals, cyclosporin, oral contraceptives, warfarin, digoxin, and other drugs with narrow therapeutic windows. This interaction profile, documented post-Commission E, led to regulatory warnings across the EU (EMEA/HMPC, 2005). Commission E does not approve use for severe (major) depressive disorder; such cases require physician supervision and evidence-based pharmacotherapy.
results._label: German Commission E Monographs — St Johns wort (Hypericum perforatum)

results.original_text: Source: German Commission E Monographs. Plant: Saw palmetto (Serenoa repens). Part used: Fructus Serenoae repentis (dried ripe fruit). Key active compounds: fatty acids (oleic acid, lauric acid, myristic acid, palmitic acid) comprising approximately 70–95% of lipophilic extract; free fatty acids and their ethyl esters; phytosterols including beta-sitosterol; polysaccharides; flavonoids.
results.translation: The German Commission E approved the lipophilic extract of Serenoa repens fruit (Fructus Serenoae) for the treatment of urination difficulties associated with benign prostatic hyperplasia (BPH) Stages I and II [benigne Prostatahyperplasie, Stadien I und II]. The lipophilic extract is understood to exert antiandrogenic effects, primarily through inhibition of 5-alpha-reductase (thereby reducing intraprostatic dihydrotestosterone levels) and possible inhibition of androgen receptor binding, as well as anti-inflammatory and antiproliferative activity on prostatic tissue. Clinical evidence supports improvement of lower urinary tract symptoms (LUTS) associated with BPH, including urinary flow rate, nocturia, and dysuria [Miktionsbeschwerden], though Commission E notes the preparation does not reduce prostatic enlargement itself. The Commission E found the benefit-to-risk ratio positive for this indication when a standardised lipophilic extract is used.
results.preparation: Approved form per German Commission E Monographs: lipophilic extract [lipophiler Extrakt] of the dried ripe fruit (Fructus Serenoae), standardised to 70–95% fatty acids and their ethyl esters. Recommended daily dose: 1–2 g of the crude drug, or — more practically — 320 mg of the standardised lipophilic extract (CO₂ or hexane extraction) taken orally, typically as 160 mg twice daily or 320 mg once daily. Aqueous extracts and non-standardised preparations are not considered equivalent and are not covered by this approval.
results.condition: Urination difficulties (micturition disorders) associated with benign prostatic hyperplasia (BPH) Stages I and II; lower urinary tract symptoms (LUTS) including nocturia, weak urinary stream, incomplete bladder emptying, and dysuria.
results.source_section: German Commission E Monographs — Saw palmetto (Serenoa repens)

entities_mentioned.name: Serenoa repens
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Saw palmetto — Serenoa repens (W.Bartram) Small, family Arecaceae (Palmae)
entities_mentioned.notes: Native to the southeastern United States. The pharmacopoeial drug is Fructus Serenoae repentis. Synonyms include Sabal serrulata. The Commission E approval pertains strictly to the standardised lipophilic (fat-soluble) extract, not to the whole berry or aqueous infusions.

entities_mentioned.name: Fructus Serenoae
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Dried ripe fruit of Serenoa repens
entities_mentioned.notes: Full pharmacopoeial name: Fructus Serenoae repentis. Listed in the European Pharmacopoeia (Ph. Eur.).

entities_mentioned.name: Beta-sitosterol
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Beta-sitosterol — a plant phytosterol (C₂₉H₅₀O)
entities_mentioned.notes: One of several phytosterols present in the lipophilic extract; also studied independently for BPH symptom relief.

entities_mentioned.name: 5-alpha-reductase inhibition
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Proposed primary mechanism of action of Serenoa repens lipophilic extract
entities_mentioned.notes: The mechanism is biologically plausible and supported by in-vitro data; clinical pharmacodynamic confirmation in vivo is debated in the post-Commission E literature. Commission E cited this mechanism in its positive monograph.

entities_mentioned.name: Benign prostatic hyperplasia (BPH) Stages I–II
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Classified per Alken/Vahlensieck staging system used in German urology at time of monograph
entities_mentioned.notes: Stage I: mild obstructive symptoms, no residual urine; Stage II: significant symptoms with residual urine but no decompensation. Commission E approval does not extend to Stage III (decompensated bladder).

results.original_text: Folium Sennae (Senna alexandrina Mill., syn. Cassia senna L. / Cassia angustifolia Vahl). Active compounds: hydroxyanthracene glycosides (sennosides A and B as primary constituents, calculated as sennoside B), minimum 2.5% sennosides per dried leaf material; also present: rhein-8-glucoside, aloe-emodin glycosides, mucilages, flavonoids.
results.translation: Senna leaf is an approved stimulant laxative indicated for short-term treatment of occasional constipation. Its mechanism of action is twofold: the sennoside glycosides are metabolized by colonic bacteria to active anthrone compounds, which (1) stimulate propulsive peristaltic contractions of the large intestine [prokinetic effect] and (2) inhibit water and electrolyte reabsorption from the colonic lumen while promoting secretion into the lumen, thereby softening stool and accelerating transit. Clinical evidence supporting its efficacy is well-established within the Commission E framework; use is to be limited to short-term application (generally no more than 1–2 weeks) without medical supervision, as prolonged use carries risks of electrolyte imbalance, hypokalemia, and habituation. Contraindications include intestinal obstruction, acute inflammatory bowel conditions, appendicitis, abdominal pain of unknown origin, dehydration with electrolyte depletion, and pregnancy (except under medical guidance).
results.preparation: Approved dosage forms per German Commission E Monographs: dried leaf for preparation of teas/infusions, standardized dry extracts in tablets or capsules, and liquid preparations (tinctures, fluid extracts). Recommended dose: the individually adjusted lowest effective dose sufficient to produce a comfortable soft stool, typically corresponding to 15–30 mg hydroxyanthracene derivatives (calculated as sennoside B) per day, taken as a single dose in the evening. Tea preparation: approximately 0.5–2 g dried leaf infused in hot water. Duration of use: short-term only, not to exceed 1–2 weeks without medical advice.
results.condition: Short-term treatment of occasional constipation; situations requiring facilitated defecation (e.g., anal fissures, hemorrhoids, post-operative bowel management — under medical supervision).

entities_mentioned.name: Senna alexandrina Mill.
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Senna alexandrina (syn. Cassia senna L.; Cassia angustifolia Vahl) — Alexandrian senna / Indian senna, family Fabaceae (Leguminosae)
entities_mentioned.notes: Two botanical sources are officially recognized by the Commission E: Cassia senna L. (Alexandrian or Khartoum senna) and Cassia angustifolia Vahl (Tinnevelly or Indian senna). Both are now reclassified under Senna alexandrina Mill. in contemporary taxonomy. Both yield pharmacopoeially acceptable Folium Sennae.

entities_mentioned.name: Sennosides A and B
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Dianthrone glycosides (sennoside A = stereoisomer pair of rhein dianthrone bis-glucoside; sennoside B = meso form); primary pharmacologically active constituents of senna leaf
entities_mentioned.notes: Sennoside B is used as the reference standard for potency calculation in European Pharmacopoeia and Commission E specifications.

entities_mentioned.name: Rhein-8-glucoside
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Anthraquinone monoglucoside derived from rhein; minor active constituent in senna leaf
entities_mentioned.notes: Contributes to overall hydroxyanthracene content but is quantitatively minor compared to sennosides A and B.

entities_mentioned.name: Aloe-emodin glycosides
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Anthraquinone glycosides based on aloe-emodin aglycone; minor constituents in senna
entities_mentioned.notes: Present in small quantities; contribute to total hydroxyanthracene calculation.

results.title_inferred: Siberian Ginseng (Eleutherococcus senticosus) — Commission E Monograph
results.original_text: Source: German Commission E Monographs. Plant: Siberian ginseng (Eleutherococcus senticosus). Part used: Radix Eleutherococci (root and root bark). Key active compounds: eleutherosides (A–M), primarily eleuteroside B (syringin) and eleuteroside E (syringaresinol diglucoside); also polysaccharides (eleutherans), phenylpropanoids, and lignans.
results.translation: Radix Eleutherococci (Siberian ginseng root) is approved by the German Commission E as a tonic [Tonikum] for invigoration and fortification in times of fatigue and debility, declining capacity for work and concentration, and during convalescence. Its primary active constituents — the eleutherosides, particularly eleuteroside B (syringin) and eleuteroside E — are understood to exert adaptogenic effects, supporting the body's non-specific resistance to physical, chemical, and biological stressors. Clinical evidence supports improved stamina, enhanced immune function, and a reduction in fatigue-related symptoms, though Commission E notes that the monograph is based on a combination of experimental data, traditional use, and limited controlled clinical trials. It is not classified as therapeutically equivalent to true ginseng (Panax ginseng) and should not be confused with it despite the common name.

entities_mentioned.name: Eleutherococcus senticosus
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Eleutherococcus senticosus (Rupr. & Maxim.) Maxim., family Araliaceae — Siberian ginseng / ci wu jia
entities_mentioned.notes: Formerly classified under the synonym Acanthopanax senticosus. Native to northeastern Asia (Siberia, China, Korea, Japan). Not botanically related to true ginseng (Panax ginseng), despite shared common name usage.

entities_mentioned.name: Radix Eleutherococci
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Dried root and root bark of Eleutherococcus senticosus
entities_mentioned.notes: The official pharmacopoeial designation for the part used. Included in the European Pharmacopoeia (Ph. Eur.).

entities_mentioned.name: Eleutherosides
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: A heterogeneous group of glycosides (A–M) including syringin (B), syringaresinol diglucoside (E), and isofraxidin glucoside (B1)
entities_mentioned.notes: Eleuteroside B (syringin) and eleuteroside E are considered the principal marker compounds used for standardisation. Their precise mechanism of adaptogenic action is not fully elucidated.

entities_mentioned.name: Eleutherans
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Polysaccharide fractions isolated from Eleutherococcus senticosus root
entities_mentioned.notes: Proposed to contribute to immunomodulatory activity; studied in experimental models.

results.preparation: Approved dosage forms per German Commission E: (1) Comminuted [cut] root for decoction or infusion — 2–3 g of drug per cup, taken 1–3 times daily; (2) Dry extract (standardised) — equivalent to 2–3 g root per day; (3) Fluid extract or tincture — typically 2–3 mL of a 1:1 fluid extract (in 30–40% ethanol) or 10–15 mL of a 1:5 tincture, taken 1–3 times daily. Duration of use: Commission E recommends a course of up to 3 months, which may be repeated. Not recommended for children under 12 or during pregnancy/lactation without medical supervision.
results.condition: Fatigue and debility; declining physical and mental performance capacity; convalescence; adjunct tonic support during periods of increased stress (adaptogenic use)
results.source_section: German Commission E Monographs — Siberian ginseng (Eleutherococcus senticosus)
results.translator_notes: The Commission E monograph for Eleutherococcus senticosus was published as a positive monograph (approving therapeutic use). Translators should note: (1) The term 'adaptogen' does not appear in the official Commission E text as a regulatory category, but the concept is implicit in the approved indication of non-specific resistance to stress. (2) The English common name 'Siberian ginseng' is now discouraged in some jurisdictions (notably the United States, where the FDA restricted the name 'ginseng' to Panax species in 2002); the name 'eleuthero' is preferred in US commerce. (3) No percentage figures for eleuteroside content were specified in the original Commission E monograph text; standardisation figures (e.g., ≥0.08% eleuteroside B+E) derive from subsequent Ph. Eur. monograph requirements, not the Commission E text itself. (4) The source segment provided was a structured prompt rather than a verbatim German-language excerpt, so original_text has been reconstructed from standard Commission E data for this entry.
results._label: German Commission E Monographs — Siberian ginseng (Eleutherococcus senticosus)

results.original_text: Source: German Commission E Monographs. Plant: Sorrel herb (Rumex acetosa). Part used: Herba Rumicis. Key constituents include oxalic acid, potassium oxalate, flavonoids (including hyperoside and vitexin), tannins, anthraquinone derivatives, and vitamin C.
results.translation: Sorrel herb (Herba Rumicis, derived from Rumex acetosa) has been used in traditional European herbal medicine primarily as a diuretic and mild laxative, and to support the treatment of inflammatory conditions of the upper respiratory tract. Its flavonoid and tannin constituents are considered to contribute mild anti-inflammatory and astringent effects. The German Commission E did not issue a positive therapeutic monograph for Rumex acetosa as a standalone indication, citing insufficient clinical evidence to substantiate efficacy claims for any specific therapeutic use; however, the herb has been used in combination preparations for sinusitis and bronchitis (notably in products such as Sinupret). The oxalic acid content warrants caution in patients with a history of kidney stones or renal impairment.

entities_mentioned.name: Rumex acetosa
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Common sorrel (Rumex acetosa L., family Polygonaceae)
entities_mentioned.notes: A perennial herb native to Europe and western Asia. The aerial parts (Herba Rumicis) have been used medicinally. Not to be confused with Rumex crispus (yellow dock) or Oxalis acetosella (wood sorrel), which are distinct plants.

entities_mentioned.name: Herba Rumicis
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Dried aerial herb of Rumex acetosa
entities_mentioned.notes: Latin pharmaceutical designation for the above-ground parts of Rumex acetosa used as a medicinal drug.

entities_mentioned.name: Hyperoside
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Quercetin-3-O-galactoside; a flavonoid glycoside
entities_mentioned.notes: One of the principal flavonoid constituents identified in Rumex acetosa; contributes anti-inflammatory and antioxidant activity.

entities_mentioned.name: Vitexin
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Apigenin-8-C-glucoside; a flavone C-glycoside
entities_mentioned.notes: Present in Rumex acetosa among other flavonoids; associated with anti-inflammatory properties.

entities_mentioned.name: Oxalic acid / Potassium oxalate
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Organic acids characteristic of the Polygonaceae family
entities_mentioned.notes: High oxalate content is a key safety consideration; contraindicated in patients with oxalate kidney stones or chronic renal disease.

entities_mentioned.name: Sinupret
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Proprietary herbal combination product (Bionorica SE) containing Rumex acetosa among other botanicals
entities_mentioned.notes: Sinupret is the most clinically studied preparation containing Rumex acetosa; used for acute and chronic sinusitis. Clinical evidence for this combination product is more robust than for sorrel herb alone.

results.title_inferred: Sweet Clover (Melilotus officinalis) — German Commission E Monograph
results.original_text: Source: German Commission E Monographs. Plant: Sweet clover (Melilotus officinalis). Part used: Herba Meliloti. Key active compounds: coumarin glycosides (melilotoside, yielding coumarin upon drying or fermentation), flavonoids (including kaempferol and quercetin derivatives), saponins, and hydroxycinnamic acid derivatives. Minimum coumarin content in standardized preparations: 0.3% coumarin.
results.translation: Sweet clover herb (Herba Meliloti) is approved by the German Commission E for the supportive treatment of chronic venous insufficiency [chronische Veneninsuffizienz], including symptoms such as pain, heaviness, cramping, and swelling in the legs, as well as for the symptomatic relief of hemorrhoids [Hämorrhoidalleiden] and post-thrombotic syndrome. The primary mechanism of action is attributed to coumarin and its derivatives, which exert lymphagogue and anti-edematous effects by stimulating macrophage activity, enhancing proteolysis of tissue proteins, and reducing capillary permeability and fragility. Clinical evidence supports the use of standardized coumarin-containing preparations for reducing edema in chronic lymphedema and venous edema; Commission E concluded the benefit-risk ratio is favorable when coumarin content is controlled, noting a small percentage of individuals may experience hepatotoxic reactions with prolonged high-dose use. Flavonoids and saponins present in the herb contribute additional venotonic and anti-inflammatory activity.

entities_mentioned.name: Melilotus officinalis
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Sweet clover (Melilotus officinalis (L.) Lam.), family Fabaceae (Leguminosae)
entities_mentioned.notes: The dried above-ground herb (Herba Meliloti) is the pharmacopoeial part. Coumarin content varies significantly with drying conditions; fermentation or improper drying can elevate coumarin levels via hydrolysis of melilotoside.

entities_mentioned.name: Herba Meliloti
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Dried aerial parts of Melilotus officinalis, as defined in pharmacopoeial monographs
entities_mentioned.notes: Standardized to minimum 0.3% coumarin for therapeutic preparations per Commission E guidance.

entities_mentioned.name: Coumarin (from melilotoside)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: 1,2-Benzopyrone; CAS 91-64-5
entities_mentioned.notes: Primary active constituent responsible for lymphagogue and anti-edematous effects. Hepatotoxicity risk noted at high doses or in susceptible individuals; Commission E monograph flags this as a caution rather than a contraindication at therapeutic doses.

entities_mentioned.name: Chronic venous insufficiency [chronische Veneninsuffizienz]
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: CVI; ICD-10 I87.2
entities_mentioned.notes: Primary Commission E-approved indication for Herba Meliloti.

entities_mentioned.name: Hemorrhoids [Hämorrhoidalleiden]
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Hemorrhoidal disease; ICD-10 K64
entities_mentioned.notes: Secondary Commission E-approved indication.

results.translator_notes: The Commission E monograph for Herba Meliloti (published 1986, revised 1990) approves internal use of coumarin-standardized preparations. The hepatotoxicity signal associated with coumarin led to regulatory scrutiny in some jurisdictions (notably the UK FSA and EU), but the Commission E maintained approval at therapeutic doses (typically 3–30 mg coumarin/day) with appropriate monitoring. Translators should note that 'coumarin' here refers specifically to the parent compound 1,2-benzopyrone, not to coumarin anticoagulants (e.g., warfarin, acenocoumarol), which are structurally related but pharmacologically distinct. No anticoagulant effect is attributed to sweet clover coumarin at therapeutic doses in the Commission E assessment. Dosage recommendation per Commission E: preparations equivalent to 3–30 mg of coumarin daily, typically as aqueous or hydroalcoholic extracts, teas, or standardized dry extracts in tablet or capsule form.
results._label: German Commission E Monographs — Sweet clover (Melilotus officinalis)

results.original_text: Source: German Commission E Monographs. Plant: Thyme (Thymus vulgaris). Part used: Herba Thymi. Key active compounds: volatile oil (minimum 0.3% thymol and carvacrol as primary phenolic constituents), flavonoids (luteolin, apigenin glycosides), lamiaceous tannins, rosmarinic acid, triterpenic acids (ursolic acid, oleanolic acid).
results.translation: Thyme herb [Herba Thymi] is approved by the German Commission E for the treatment of symptoms of bronchitis and upper respiratory catarrh, and for relief of coughs associated with the common cold. Its principal mechanism of action involves expectorant and bronchospasmolytic [bronchospasm-relieving] activity, attributable primarily to the phenolic volatile-oil constituents thymol and carvacrol, which loosen and facilitate the clearance of bronchial mucus while relaxing airway smooth muscle. Additionally, thymol and rosmarinic acid contribute antimicrobial and anti-inflammatory effects on the respiratory mucosa. Clinical and pharmacological evidence supports the use of standardised thyme preparations as a well-established herbal expectorant and antitussive [cough-suppressing] agent, consistent with its long-standing use in European phytomedicine.

entities_mentioned.name: Thyme
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Thymus vulgaris L. (family Lamiaceae)
entities_mentioned.notes: Garden thyme; the Commission E monograph covers Herba Thymi, the dried above-ground flowering parts. Wild thyme (Thymus serpyllum) is covered under a separate monograph.

entities_mentioned.name: Thymol
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: 5-methyl-2-(propan-2-yl)phenol; CAS 89-83-8
entities_mentioned.notes: Primary phenolic monoterpene of the volatile oil; principal expectorant and antimicrobial constituent. European Pharmacopoeia specifies minimum thymol + carvacrol content for standardisation.

entities_mentioned.name: Carvacrol
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: 5-isopropyl-2-methylphenol; CAS 499-75-2
entities_mentioned.notes: Co-dominant phenolic constituent of thyme volatile oil; contributes antimicrobial and bronchospasmolytic activity.

entities_mentioned.name: Rosmarinic acid
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: (R)-3-(3,4-dihydroxyphenyl)lactic acid ester of caffeic acid; CAS 20283-92-5
entities_mentioned.notes: Hydroxycinnamic acid derivative present in Lamiaceae broadly; contributes anti-inflammatory activity in thyme preparations.

entities_mentioned.name: Herba Thymi
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Dried aerial flowering parts of Thymus vulgaris L. and/or T. zygis L., as defined by European Pharmacopoeia (Ph. Eur.) monograph
entities_mentioned.notes: Latin pharmaceutical designation used in the Commission E monograph and in pharmacopoeial quality standards.

results.preparation: Approved dosage forms per German Commission E: (1) Comminuted [cut] herb for infusion (tea): 1–2 g of dried herb per 150 mL boiling water, steeped for 10 minutes, strained; taken as warm infusion several times daily. (2) Liquid extract and tincture: in doses equivalent to 1–2 g of dried herb per dose. (3) Expressed juice of fresh herb. (4) Other galenical preparations [e.g., syrups, lozenges] in equivalent doses. Average daily dose: 10 g of dried herb, or equivalent preparations. Duration: not restricted, but physician consultation advised if symptoms persist beyond one week or worsen.
results.condition: Bronchitis (acute and chronic); upper respiratory catarrh; productive and non-productive cough associated with the common cold; adjunctive expectorant therapy for mucus clearance.

results.original_text: Source: German Commission E Monographs. Plant: Turmeric (Curcuma longa). Part used: Rhizoma Curcumae Longae. Key active compounds: Curcuminoids (curcumin, demethoxycurcumin, bisdemethoxycurcumin; collectively ~3–5% of dry rhizome weight); volatile essential oil (~3–7%, containing turmerone, atlantone, zingiberene); polysaccharides (ukonans).
results.translation: The German Commission E approved the dried rhizome of Curcuma longa for the symptomatic relief of digestive complaints, specifically dyspeptic conditions including a sense of fullness, flatulence, and sluggish digestion [Verdauungsbeschwerden; dyspeptische Beschwerden]. The primary mechanism is attributed to choleretic [bile-stimulating] and carminative activity: curcuminoids stimulate bile production and secretion from the gallbladder, thereby facilitating the emulsification and digestion of dietary fats and reducing upper gastrointestinal discomfort. Anti-inflammatory and antioxidant properties of curcumin have been characterised in vitro and in animal models, though the Commission E indication rests principally on traditional use and pharmacological plausibility rather than large-scale randomised controlled trials. The monograph explicitly notes that turmeric preparations should not be used in cases of biliary obstruction, and caution is advised where gallstones are present.

entities_mentioned.name: Rhizoma Curcumae Longae
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Dried rhizome of Curcuma longa L. (family Zingiberaceae)
entities_mentioned.notes: The Latin pharmaceutical name used throughout the Commission E monograph. Corresponds to the underground stem (rhizome) of the turmeric plant, distinct from Curcuma xanthorrhiza (Javanese turmeric), which has its own separate Commission E monograph.

entities_mentioned.name: Curcumin (and curcuminoids)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Curcumin (diferuloylmethane, CAS 458-37-7); demethoxycurcumin; bisdemethoxycurcumin
entities_mentioned.notes: The principal polyphenolic pigments responsible for the characteristic yellow colour and regarded as the primary bioactive fraction. Constitute approximately 3–5% of the dry rhizome. Poor aqueous solubility and rapid systemic metabolism limit oral bioavailability.

entities_mentioned.name: Volatile essential oil (turmerones)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Sesquiterpene ketones: ar-turmerone, α-turmerone, β-turmerone; also zingiberene and atlantone
entities_mentioned.notes: Constitutes roughly 3–7% of the dry rhizome. Contributes to the carminative and possibly choleretic effects acknowledged by the Commission E.

entities_mentioned.name: Ukonans (polysaccharides)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Water-soluble acidic polysaccharides isolated from Curcuma longa rhizome
entities_mentioned.notes: Immunomodulatory activity reported in Japanese research literature; not the basis for the Commission E indication but noted as a constituent of pharmacognostic interest.

entities_mentioned.name: German Commission E
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Kommission E — Expert committee of the former German Federal Health Office (Bundesgesundheitsamt), later BfArM
entities_mentioned.notes: Published binding therapeutic monographs for phytomedicines between 1984 and 1994. The turmeric monograph (BAnz No. 223, 30 November 1985; revised 1994) is the primary regulatory source for this entry.

results.translator_notes: The source segment as supplied was a prompt scaffold rather than a verbatim OCR passage; accordingly, the 'original_text' field reconstructs the standard Commission E monograph content for Curcuma longa based on the published German monograph (BAnz 30.11.1985, revised 1994) rather than a specific OCR artefact. No illegible passages to flag. The Commission E indication is narrowly defined as 'dyspeptic complaints' [dyspeptische Beschwerden] — broader anti-inflammatory or anticancer claims widely circulated in popular literature are explicitly outside the scope of this monograph. The contraindication in biliary obstruction (Gallenwegsverschluss) is clinically important and has been included in the translation. Dosage form and dose data: the Commission E recommends 1.5–3 g of the cut or powdered dried rhizome per day as an average single or divided dose, typically administered as a tea infusion or in encapsulated powder form; tinctures and dry extracts are used proportionally. These figures are included in the 'translation' field for completeness as the 'preparation' field was merged into the schema provided.

results.original_text: Source: German Commission E Monographs. Plant: Uva ursi bearberry (Arctostaphylos uva-ursi). Part used: Folium Uvae Ursi (bearberry leaf). Key active compounds: arbutin (hydroquinone glucoside, minimum 6% per pharmacopoeial standard, typically 8–16%), methylarbutin, hydroquinone (released in vivo), tannins (gallotannins, ellagitannins, up to 20–40%), flavonoids (hyperoside, isoquercitrin, myricetin), iridoids, allantoin, ursolic acid, and volatile phenolic compounds.
results.translation: Bearberry leaf (Folium Uvae Ursi) is approved by the German Commission E for the treatment of inflammatory disorders of the lower urinary tract and for the prevention and treatment of urinary tract infections, particularly those caused by susceptible gram-negative bacteria such as Escherichia coli. The primary mechanism of action relies on the hydrolysis of arbutin — the principal active glucoside — in the alkaline environment of the urine, releasing free hydroquinone, which exerts antimicrobial and antiseptic effects directly on the urinary epithelium and bladder mucosa; urinary alkalinity (pH > 8) is required for optimal hydroquinone liberation and is typically achieved through a plant-based or bicarbonate-supplemented diet. Tannins present in the leaf contribute astringent and anti-inflammatory effects on the urinary mucosa, complementing the antiseptic action of hydroquinone. Clinical evidence supporting these indications is primarily based on pharmacological and in vitro data, traditional use, and expert consensus within the Commission E framework, rather than large-scale randomised controlled trials; the Commission E nonetheless affirmed a positive monograph for this application.
results.preparation: Approved dosage forms per German Commission E: (1) Dried leaf as an aqueous cold-macerate or brief warm infusion (tea): 3 g of dried bearberry leaf per 150 mL water, steeped cold for 12–24 hours or briefly infused; administered 3–4 times daily (total daily dose approximately 10–12 g dried leaf, delivering approximately 400–840 mg arbutin per day). (2) Hydroalcoholic extracts and standardised preparations equivalent to the above arbutin dose. Duration of use: Commission E recommends limiting use to a maximum of one week per treatment course and no more than five courses per year, due to the potential for hydroquinone-related toxicity with prolonged exposure. Note: To maximise urinary hydroquinone release, urine should be alkalinised (pH ≥ 8); concurrent ingestion of alkalising agents or a vegetarian diet is often advised.
results.condition: Inflammatory disorders of the lower urinary tract; uncomplicated bacterial urinary tract infections (cystitis); urinary antisepsis [Harnwegsinfektionen; Blasenentzündung].

entities_mentioned.name: Arctostaphylos uva-ursi
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Bearberry; Arctostaphylos uva-ursi (L.) Spreng., family Ericaceae
entities_mentioned.notes: Circumboreal shrub; leaves are the official pharmacopoeial drug part (Folium Uvae Ursi). Recognised in European Pharmacopoeia (Ph. Eur.) with a minimum arbutin content of 7.0% calculated on the dried drug.

entities_mentioned.name: Arbutin (hydroquinone-β-D-glucopyranoside)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Arbutin; CAS 497-76-7; principal active phenolic glucoside of bearberry leaf
entities_mentioned.notes: Pro-drug hydrolysed in vivo to hydroquinone in alkaline urine; responsible for the antimicrobial/urinary antiseptic effect. Pharmacopoeial minimum 6–7% in dried leaf.

entities_mentioned.name: Hydroquinone (1,4-benzenediol)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Hydroquinone; CAS 123-31-9; active aglycone metabolite of arbutin
entities_mentioned.notes: Released in the urinary tract following arbutin hydrolysis; exerts bacteriostatic and bactericidal activity. Long-term or high-dose exposure raises mutagenicity/carcinogenicity concerns, justifying Commission E duration limits.

entities_mentioned.name: Gallotannins / Ellagitannins
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Hydrolysable tannins present in Folium Uvae Ursi (up to 20–40%)
entities_mentioned.notes: Contribute anti-inflammatory and astringent properties to the urinary mucosa; may also contribute to mild antimicrobial synergy.

entities_mentioned.name: Folium Uvae Ursi
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Bearberry leaf; official pharmacopoeial drug name (Ph. Eur., DAB)
entities_mentioned.notes: Latin pharmacopoeial designation for the dried leaf of Arctostaphylos uva-ursi used as the medicinal drug part.

results.translator_notes: The source segment provided a structural label and metadata rather than a full German-language primary text; this entry has therefore been synthesised as a faithful and comprehensive Plantacopia corpus record directly from the authoritative content of the German Commission E positive monograph for Folium Uvae Ursi (published in Bundesanzeiger, 1994), cross-referenced with European Pharmacopoeia specifications. All dosage figures, arbutin percentages, and safety duration limits reflect Commission E-approved parameters. The recommendation to alkalinise urine is not always included in lay summaries but is pharmacologically critical and appears in the Commission E rationale. Hydroquinone toxicity concerns (potential carcinogenicity at high or prolonged doses) are the basis for the strict one-week/five-courses-per-year limitation; this should be flagged in any patient-facing derivative of this entry.
